papers.full_text: Maintenance of Hokkaido virus, a genotype of Orthohantavirus puumalaense, in the rodent host Myodes rufocanus bedfordiae under natural conditions ABSTRACT A variety of orthohantaviruses (family Hantaviridae) have preferred natural host species, with transmission among hosts generally thought to occur through direct physical contact and inhalation of virus-contaminated excreta, although the infection to the other species occasionally occurs. Despite extensive experimental studies, the mechanisms of orthohantavirus maintenance and transmission under natural conditions remain unclear. In this study, field surveys were conducted in a forest in Tobetsu, Hokkaido, Japan, between 2022 and 2025 to capture gray red-backed voles (Myodes rufocanus bedfordiae), the natural host of Hokkaido virus (HOKV), a genotype of Orthohantavirus puumalaense. Among 199 captured rodents, 23 were positive for HOKV infection. Five individuals were positive for viral RNA but negative for anti-HOKV IgG antibodies on ELISA and IFA and exhibited low neutralizing antibody titers and low IgG avidity indexes (≤26%), suggesting acute infection. In contrast, 18 individuals were positive for viral RNA and showed high antibody titers on ELISA, IFA, and neutralization tests, as well as high IgG avidities (≥64%); these individuals were considered persistently infected. High levels of viral RNA and antigens were consistently detected in the lungs, kidneys, and spleen during both potential acute and persistent phases of HOKV infection by quantitative PCR and immunohistochemistry. Infectious HOKV was also recovered from oral swabs (8/8), urine (3/6), and feces (4/6) of individual rodents captured in 2024. These findings showed that HOKV can persist at high viral loads in host organs and be excreted throughout the course of infection, contributing to the long-term maintenance of orthohantavirus in natural host populations. IMPORTANCE Diseases caused by zoonotic agents are major public health concerns. Orthohantaviruses are typical examples of zoonotic viruses transmitted from wild rodent hosts. Despite extensive laboratory investigations, the mechanisms of viral persistence and transmission in natural hosts remain poorly understood. Our study demonstrated that Hokkaido virus (HOKV), a genotype of Orthohantavirus puumalaense, seems to establish both acute and persistent infections in wild grey red-backed voles (Myodes rufocanus bedfordiae) without inducing major pathological changes. Detection of infectious virus in saliva, urine, and feces revealed multiple virus-shedding routes. Saliva likely serves as the predominant source of transmission, given that infectious virus was recovered from oral swabs of all infected rodents captured in 2024. These findings expand understanding of orthohantavirus ecology, persistence, and maintenance in reservoir populations. This information is crucial for evaluating spillover risks and enhancing public health preparedness. INTRODUCTION Orthohantaviruses, members of the genus Orthohantavirus within the family Hantaviridae, are a diverse group of single-stranded, negative-sense RNA viruses. These viruses have a three-segmented RNA genome encoding the nucleocapsid (N) and, in some cases, nonstructural (NSs) proteins on the small (S) RNA segment, envelope glycoproteins (Gn and Gc) on the medium (M) RNA segment, and RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) on the large (L) RNA segment. Thirty-eight Orthohantavirus species have been identified to date, some of which are known to cause severe disease in humans and pose public health risks worldwide. Recent studies have identified a broader host range for hantaviruses, including shrews, moles, and bats. Host sharing, host-switching, and spillover events have occurred repeatedly during the evolutionary history of hantaviruses and have contributed to their complex evolutionary patterns and phylogeographic structure. Recently, it has been revealed that some orthohantavirus can infect multiple species of rodents. Nevertheless, each orthohantavirus has a preferred host species and is considered to maintain a long-term reservoir host–virus relationship. For example, Orthohantavirus hantanense (Hantaan virus, HTNV), Orthohantavirus seoulense (Seoul virus, SEOV), Orthohantavirus puumalaense (Puumala virus, PUUV), Orthohantavirus andesense (Andes virus, ANDV), and Orthohantavirus sinnombreense (Sin Nombre virus, SNV) are primarily carried by the striped field mouse (Apodemus agrarius), Norway rat (Rattus norvegicus), bank vole (Myodes glareolus), long-tailed pygmy rice rat (Oligoryzomys longicaudatus), and deer mouse (Peromyscus maniculatus), respectively. Orthohantaviruses are believed to infect humans through inhalation of virus-containing excreta from infected animals or through animal bites. Human infections caused by orthohantaviruses are clinically classified into two main disease types: hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS), predominantly reported in Eurasia, and hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HPS), occurring mainly in North and South America. Both diseases have high rates of morbidity and mortality. In contrast to their pathogenicity in humans, previous studies have shown that orthohantaviruses typically persist without symptoms in their natural hosts, with detectable viral RNA in multiple tissues and viral maintenance lasting for over a year, even in the presence of a specific immune response. Transmission among hosts is generally assumed to occur via direct physical contact, particularly biting involving virus-bearing saliva, or by inhalation of infectious aerosols generated from contaminated excreta. However, most data reported to date were derived from studies on experimentally infected animals, and limited information is available on the mode of orthohantavirus infection in individual rodents or the mechanisms of transmission among host populations in natural environments. Thus, field-based investigations are crucial for understanding how these viruses are maintained in their host populations over long periods, including aspects of virus tissue tropism, persistence in infected animals, and routes of excretion. Hokkaido virus (HOKV) is a genotype of O. puumalaense and was first identified over 30 years ago in Tobetsu, Hokkaido, Japan, from a wild gray red-backed vole (Myodes rufocanus bedfordiae), a subspecies of Myodes rufocanus. M. rufocanus is geographically distributed across the northern Palearctic region, extending from northern Fennoscandia through Russia to northern Asia, including Hokkaido, Japan. HOKV has been detected in several regions across this distribution range. The HOKV lineages found in Hokkaido, Japan, and Sakhalin, Russia, are genetically close to PUUV strains hosted by M. glareolus. However, no human infections with HOKV have been reported to date, in contrast to PUUV, which has been shown to cause HFRS in Europe and Russia. Although M. rufocanus bedfordiae is a natural reservoir host of HOKV, little is known about the characteristics of HOKV-infected individuals and the ecology of HOKV within the rodent population. In this study, we conducted field surveys in Tobetsu, Hokkaido, Japan, from 2022 to 2025, and examined viral loads in various internal organs and excreta of naturally infected rodents. Our findings showed that orthohantaviruses can persist in host organs at high levels and be shed through excreta during both potentially acute and persistent phases of infection. MATERIALS AND METHODS Wild rodent sampling A field investigation was conducted four times within a windbreak forest located in Tobetsu, Hokkaido, Japan, from 2022 to 2025 (43°12′21′′ N, 141°25′18′′ E), where HOKV is known to circulate among M. rufocanus bedfordiae populations. Wild rodents were captured using 100 Sherman live traps baited with oatmeal that were set overnight at each survey. A total of 199 rodents were captured and identified using a combination of morphological characteristics and nucleotide sequence analysis of the mitochondrial D-loop region. The rodent identification numbers were presented in the format of individual number/year. Under isoflurane anesthesia (Abbott Laboratories, Abbott Park, IL, USA), blood was collected via cardiac puncture. Urine, feces, and oral swab specimens were obtained, and internal organs (including the lungs, liver, spleen, kidneys, heart, salivary glands, and rectum) were collected and divided into two portions: one part was fixed in 10% neutral-buffered formalin (Wako Pure Chemicals, Osaka, Japan), and the other part was preserved in cryovials. All biological samples were immediately stored on ice and transported to the laboratory for further examination. Samples in formalin and cryovials were stored at room temperature (RT) and −80°C, respectively, until use. Sample preparation was performed in a biosafety level 3 containment laboratory, except for sample collections from animals that were carried out in the field. Serological screening for anti-orthohantavirus antibodies Heat-inactivated (56°C, 30 min) serum samples from all captured rodents were screened for the presence of IgG antibodies against orthohantavirus antigens by indirect immunofluorescent antibody assay (IFA) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) as described previously. Indirect immunofluorescent antibody assay for detection of IgG (IgG-IFA) and IgM (IgM-IFA) Monolayers of Vero E6 cells were infected with the PUUV strain Samara_94/CG/2009 at MOI of 0.01 and incubated for 14 days. The infected cells were harvested by trypsinization and spotted onto 24-well slides. After incubation for 4 h, the cells were fixed with methanol for 20 min, air-dried, and stored at −30°C until use as antigen slides. Rodent sera were diluted serially by two-fold (starting at 1:16) and tested for anti-hantavirus IgG antibodies using the antigen slides and Alexa Fluor 488-labeled protein G (1:500; Invitrogen). Monoclonal antibody E5/G6 was used as the positive control. For IgM detection, Alexa Fluor 488-conjugated anti-mouse IgM μ chain (1:500; Abcam, Cambridge, UK) was used. Sera from HOKV-infected hamsters were used as the positive controls. Scattered granular fluorescence in the cytoplasm was considered a positive reaction. IFA titers were determined as the reciprocal of the maximum dilution of serum that produced a positive signal. Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay for IgG detection (IgG-ELISA) Recombinant N protein (rNP) of HOKV was expressed as a fusion protein with the N-utilization substance A (NusA) protein, which was used as the specific antigen. The flat-bottom 96-well microtiter plates (Corning Inc., Corning, NY, USA) were coated overnight at 4°C with 50 μL per well of the rNP or the NusA diluted in 0.05 M carbonate/bicarbonate buffer (pH 9.8; Sigma) at a concentration of 1.6 μg/mL. The coated plates were blocked with a 1:5 dilution of Block Ace (Dai Nippon Pharmaceutical, Osaka, Japan) in deionized distilled water (DDW) and incubated at 37°C for 1 h. After washing three times with phosphate-buffered saline containing 0.05% Tween 20 (PBST), 2-fold serial dilutions from 1:100 to 1:6400 of serum samples were added to the plates (50 μL/well) and incubated at 37°C for 1 h. Each serum sample was reacted with the rN and the NusA protein. After the plates were washed three times with PBST, protein G–peroxidase (PO) conjugate (1:500; Zymed, San Francisco, CA, USA) was applied to detect antibodies (50 μL/well), and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) tablets (Sigma, St. Louis, MO, USA), together with hydrogen peroxide, served as the substrate for the peroxidase reaction (100 μL/well). The optical density at 450 nm (OD450) was measured using a plate reader (Multiskan MS; Labsystems Diagnostics Oy, Helsinki, Finland). The OD value of the rNP well minus that of the NusA well for the same serum was calculated and regarded as the ELISA value. ELISA titer was expressed as the reciprocal of the highest dilution showing an OD value of 0.2 or higher than 0.2. The cut-off value (0.2) was defined as the mean of negative sera plus three standard deviations (mean + 3 SD). Monoclonal antibody (mAb) E5/G6 to orthohantavirus N protein was used as the positive control. IgG avidity assay by ELISA IgG avidity was determined using a modified ELISA assay based on the previously described protocols. Briefly, diluted serum samples (1:100) were added to HOKV rNP- and NusA protein-coated wells and incubated under the same conditions as described for the ELISA assay. Each serum sample was tested in quadruplicate wells. Following incubation, one set of duplicate wells was washed three times sequentially with PBST, PBST containing 6 M urea, and PBST, whereas the corresponding duplicate wells were washed three times with PBST alone. After washing, the wells were incubated with protein A/G–peroxidase (PO) conjugate (Zymed, San Francisco, CA, USA), followed by the addition of the chromogenic substrate o-phenylenediamine (OPD) (Sigma, St. Louis, MO, USA). The optical density was measured at 450 nm (OD450). The OD value of the rNP well minus that of the NusA well with the same serum was calculated and regarded as the ELISA value. IgG avidity index was expressed as the percentage of the ELISA values obtained from the urea-washed wells to those from the PBST-washed wells. Molecular screening for viral RNA by RT-PCR Lung tissues from all rodents were used for detection of viral RNA by reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR). Total RNA from lungs was extracted using an ISOGEN II Kit (Nippon Gene, Tokyo, Japan). A partial sequence of the HOKV S-segment gene was amplified using the forward primer HOK_S_180Fw (5′-acggcaacaaacagtgtcag-3′), the reverse primer HOK_S_514Rv (5′-ggatcctcgtccctttgttt-3′), and the PrimeScript One-Step RT-PCR Kit (Takara Shuzo, Kyoto, Japan). Cell culture MRK101 cells, a cell line derived from the kidney of M. rufocanus bedfordiae established previously, were maintained in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM; high glucose; Wako Pure Chemicals) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS; Biowest, Nuaillé, France), 100 IU/mL penicillin, and 100 μg/mL streptomycin (both from Nacalai Tesque, Kyoto, Japan) at 37°C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 and subcultured every 3–4 days. Virus titration Infectious viral loads in oral swabs, urine, and feces from rodents or in culture supernatants after virus propagation were quantified using a focus-forming unit (FFU) assay, as described previously. Samples were first subjected to serial dilution in DMEM and then inoculated onto confluent MRK101 cells cultured in 96-well plates. After adsorption at 37°C for 1 h, the inoculum was removed, and the cells were overlaid with DMEM supplemented with 1.5% carboxymethyl cellulose sodium salt (Wako Pure Chemicals) and 2% FBS. Infected monolayers were fixed 10 days post-infection with 100 μL of methanol per well for 15 min and then air-dried at RT. Viral foci were stained using the 2H10 mAb targeting the orthohantavirus nucleocapsid (N) protein, followed by Alexa Fluor 488-conjugated anti-mouse IgG (1:1000; Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA, USA). Stained foci were counted under a fluorescence microscope. Oral swab, urine, and feces samples were diluted or suspended in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), filtered through a 0.45 μm pore-size filter (Sartorius Stedim Biotech, Göttingen, Germany), and then used for virus titration assays. Focus reduction neutralization test Neutralizing antibody titers were measured by focus reduction neutralization test (FRNT) as described previously. Heat-inactivated rodent serum samples were serially diluted in DMEM. Equal volumes of diluted serum and virus stock solution (2.58 × 103 FFU/mL) were mixed and incubated at 37°C for 1 h. Thereafter, the virus–serum mixture was inoculated onto a monolayer of MRK101 cells grown in 96-well plates (50 μL/well). After 1 h of incubation at 37°C, the inoculum was removed; cells were overlaid with DMEM containing 1.5% carboxymethyl cellulose sodium salt (Wako Pure Chemicals) and cultured in a humidified incubator at 37°C with 5% CO2 for 10 days. Following incubation, the cells were washed and fixed with methanol. Viral foci were visualized with 50 μL of mAb E5/G6, followed by Alexa Fluor 488-conjugated anti-mouse IgG (1:1000; Invitrogen). The FRNT titer of each serum sample was defined as the reciprocal of the highest serum dilution that produced at least an 80% reduction in foci number compared with the virus control. RNA extraction Total RNA from tissues was extracted using an ISOGEN II Kit (Nippon Gene, Tokyo, Japan) in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions. For oral swabs, urine, and feces, a High Pure Viral RNA Kit (Roche, Mannheim, Germany) was used. Approximately 50 mg of feces were homogenized in 500 μL of PBS, vortexed, and centrifuged at 4°C for 10 min at 4,000 rpm. Subsequently, 200 μL of the resulting supernatant was mixed with 400 μL of binding buffer containing linear polyacrylamide (5 ng/μL). Urine samples (50 μL) were diluted in 150 μL of PBS and then mixed with 400 μL of binding buffer containing linear polyacrylamide (5 ng/μL). Similarly, 200 μL aliquots of oral swab suspensions were directly combined with 400 μL of binding buffer containing linear polyacrylamide (5 ng/μL). All preparations were vortexed for 10 s, incubated at RT for 10 min, and processed via High Pure Filter Tubes (Roche), according to the manufacturer’s protocol. RNA was eluted in 50 μL of RNase-free water, quantified with a NanoDrop One Microvolume Spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA), and stored at –80°C until analysis. cDNA synthesis and qPCR analysis First-strand cDNA was synthesized from total RNA using a PrimeScript II First-Strand cDNA Synthesis Kit (Takara Shuzo, Kyoto, Japan) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. An aliquot of 1 μL of the synthesized cDNA served as the template for qPCR using a KAPA Probe Fast qPCR Master Mix (2×) Kit (Nippon Gene), a…

full_text_sections.full_text: Maintenance of Hokkaido virus, a genotype of Orthohantavirus puumalaense, in the rodent host Myodes rufocanus bedfordiae under natural conditions ABSTRACT A variety of orthohantaviruses (family Hantaviridae) have preferred natural host species, with transmission among hosts generally thought to occur through direct physical contact and inhalation of virus-contaminated excreta, although the infection to the other species occasionally occurs. Despite extensive experimental studies, the mechanisms of orthohantavirus maintenance and transmission under natural conditions remain unclear. In this study, field surveys were conducted in a forest in Tobetsu, Hokkaido, Japan, between 2022 and 2025 to capture gray red-backed voles (Myodes rufocanus bedfordiae), the natural host of Hokkaido virus (HOKV), a genotype of Orthohantavirus puumalaense. Among 199 captured rodents, 23 were positive for HOKV infection. Five individuals were positive for viral RNA but negative for anti-HOKV IgG antibodies on ELISA and IFA and exhibited low neutralizing antibody titers and low IgG avidity indexes (≤26%), suggesting acute infection. In contrast, 18 individuals were positive for viral RNA and showed high antibody titers on ELISA, IFA, and neutralization tests, as well as high IgG avidities (≥64%); these individuals were considered persistently infected. High levels of viral RNA and antigens were consistently detected in the lungs, kidneys, and spleen during both potential acute and persistent phases of HOKV infection by quantitative PCR and immunohistochemistry. Infectious HOKV was also recovered from oral swabs (8/8), urine (3/6), and feces (4/6) of individual rodents captured in 2024. These findings showed that HOKV can persist at high viral loads in host organs and be excreted throughout the course of infection, contributing to the long-term maintenance of orthohantavirus in natural host populations. IMPORTANCE Diseases caused by zoonotic agents are major public health concerns. Orthohantaviruses are typical examples of zoonotic viruses transmitted from wild rodent hosts. Despite extensive laboratory investigations, the mechanisms of viral persistence and transmission in natural hosts remain poorly understood. Our study demonstrated that Hokkaido virus (HOKV), a genotype of Orthohantavirus puumalaense, seems to establish both acute and persistent infections in wild grey red-backed voles (Myodes rufocanus bedfordiae) without inducing major pathological changes. Detection of infectious virus in saliva, urine, and feces revealed multiple virus-shedding routes. Saliva likely serves as the predominant source of transmission, given that infectious virus was recovered from oral swabs of all infected rodents captured in 2024. These findings expand understanding of orthohantavirus ecology, persistence, and maintenance in reservoir populations. This information is crucial for evaluating spillover risks and enhancing public health preparedness. INTRODUCTION Orthohantaviruses, members of the genus Orthohantavirus within the family Hantaviridae, are a diverse group of single-stranded, negative-sense RNA viruses. These viruses have a three-segmented RNA genome encoding the nucleocapsid (N) and, in some cases, nonstructural (NSs) proteins on the small (S) RNA segment, envelope glycoproteins (Gn and Gc) on the medium (M) RNA segment, and RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) on the large (L) RNA segment. Thirty-eight Orthohantavirus species have been identified to date, some of which are known to cause severe disease in humans and pose public health risks worldwide. Recent studies have identified a broader host range for hantaviruses, including shrews, moles, and bats. Host sharing, host-switching, and spillover events have occurred repeatedly during the evolutionary history of hantaviruses and have contributed to their complex evolutionary patterns and phylogeographic structure. Recently, it has been revealed that some orthohantavirus can infect multiple species of rodents. Nevertheless, each orthohantavirus has a preferred host species and is considered to maintain a long-term reservoir host–virus relationship. For example, Orthohantavirus hantanense (Hantaan virus, HTNV), Orthohantavirus seoulense (Seoul virus, SEOV), Orthohantavirus puumalaense (Puumala virus, PUUV), Orthohantavirus andesense (Andes virus, ANDV), and Orthohantavirus sinnombreense (Sin Nombre virus, SNV) are primarily carried by the striped field mouse (Apodemus agrarius), Norway rat (Rattus norvegicus), bank vole (Myodes glareolus), long-tailed pygmy rice rat (Oligoryzomys longicaudatus), and deer mouse (Peromyscus maniculatus), respectively. Orthohantaviruses are believed to infect humans through inhalation of virus-containing excreta from infected animals or through animal bites. Human infections caused by orthohantaviruses are clinically classified into two main disease types: hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS), predominantly reported in Eurasia, and hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HPS), occurring mainly in North and South America. Both diseases have high rates of morbidity and mortality. In contrast to their pathogenicity in humans, previous studies have shown that orthohantaviruses typically persist without symptoms in their natural hosts, with detectable viral RNA in multiple tissues and viral maintenance lasting for over a year, even in the presence of a specific immune response. Transmission among hosts is generally assumed to occur via direct physical contact, particularly biting involving virus-bearing saliva, or by inhalation of infectious aerosols generated from contaminated excreta. However, most data reported to date were derived from studies on experimentally infected animals, and limited information is available on the mode of orthohantavirus infection in individual rodents or the mechanisms of transmission among host populations in natural environments. Thus, field-based investigations are crucial for understanding how these viruses are maintained in their host populations over long periods, including aspects of virus tissue tropism, persistence in infected animals, and routes of excretion. Hokkaido virus (HOKV) is a genotype of O. puumalaense and was first identified over 30 years ago in Tobetsu, Hokkaido, Japan, from a wild gray red-backed vole (Myodes rufocanus bedfordiae), a subspecies of Myodes rufocanus. M. rufocanus is geographically distributed across the northern Palearctic region, extending from northern Fennoscandia through Russia to northern Asia, including Hokkaido, Japan. HOKV has been detected in several regions across this distribution range. The HOKV lineages found in Hokkaido, Japan, and Sakhalin, Russia, are genetically close to PUUV strains hosted by M. glareolus. However, no human infections with HOKV have been reported to date, in contrast to PUUV, which has been shown to cause HFRS in Europe and Russia. Although M. rufocanus bedfordiae is a natural reservoir host of HOKV, little is known about the characteristics of HOKV-infected individuals and the ecology of HOKV within the rodent population. In this study, we conducted field surveys in Tobetsu, Hokkaido, Japan, from 2022 to 2025, and examined viral loads in various internal organs and excreta of naturally infected rodents. Our findings showed that orthohantaviruses can persist in host organs at high levels and be shed through excreta during both potentially acute and persistent phases of infection. MATERIALS AND METHODS Wild rodent sampling A field investigation was conducted four times within a windbreak forest located in Tobetsu, Hokkaido, Japan, from 2022 to 2025 (43°12′21′′ N, 141°25′18′′ E), where HOKV is known to circulate among M. rufocanus bedfordiae populations. Wild rodents were captured using 100 Sherman live traps baited with oatmeal that were set overnight at each survey. A total of 199 rodents were captured and identified using a combination of morphological characteristics and nucleotide sequence anal…

analysis.background: A variety of orthohantaviruses have preferred natural hosts and are transmitted mainly by contact and inhalation of contaminated excreta, but the mechanisms of maintenance in natural settings remain unclear.
analysis.methods: Field surveys were conducted in a forest in Tobetsu, Hokkaido, Japan, between 2022 and 2025 to capture gray red‑backed voles (Myodes rufocanus bedfordiae); 199 rodents were examined for viral RNA and antibodies using qPCR, ELISA, IFA, neutralization tests, IgG avidity assays, immunohistochemistry, and virus isolation from oral swabs, urine, and feces.
analysis.results: 23 of 199 rodents were HOKV positive; 5 were RNA‑positive but antibody‑negative (acute infection), 18 were RNA‑positive with high antibody titers and avidity (persistent infection); viral RNA and antigens were detected in lungs, kidneys, and spleen; infectious virus was recovered from all oral swabs, 3/6 urine, and 4/6 feces of 2024 captures.

analysis.summary_en: Background: A variety of orthohantaviruses have preferred natural hosts and are transmitted mainly by contact and inhalation of contaminated excreta, but the mechanisms of maintenance in natural settings remain unclear. Methods: Field surveys were conducted in a forest in Tobetsu, Hokkaido, Japan, between 2022 and 2025 to capture gray red‑backed voles (Myodes rufocanus bedfordiae); 199 rodents were examined for viral RNA and antibodies using qPCR, ELISA, IFA, neutralization tests, IgG avidity assays, immunohistochemistry, and virus isolation from oral swabs, urine, and feces. Results: 23 of 199 rodents were HOKV positive; 5 were RNA‑positive but antibody‑negative (acute infection), 18 were RNA‑positive with high antibody titers and avidity (persistent infection); viral RNA and antigens were detected in lungs, kidneys, and spleen; infectious virus was recovered from all oral swabs, 3/6 urine, and 4/6 feces of 2024 captures. Contribution: The study demonstrates that HOKV establishes both acute and persistent infections in its natural host without major pathology, shows multiple shedding routes with saliva as a likely predominant source, and expands understanding of hantavirus ecology and maintenance, informing spillover risk assessment. papers.title_zh: 在自然条件下,Hokkaido 病毒(正汉坦病毒 puumalaense 基因型)在啮齿动物宿主 Myodes rufocanus bedfordiae 中的维持。

analysis_zh.background: 多种正汉坦病毒es具有首选的自然宿主,主要通过接触和吸入受污染的排泄物传播,但其在自然环境中的维持机制仍不清楚。
analysis_zh.methods: 在2022至2025年间,于日本北海道登别的森林开展野外调查,捕获灰背红背田鼠(Myodes rufocanus bedfordiae);对199只啮齿动物使用qPCR、ELISA、IFA、中和试验、IgG亲和力测定、免疫组织化学以及从口腔拭子、尿液和粪便中分离病毒,对其病毒RNA和抗体进行检测。
analysis_zh.results: 199只啮齿动物中有23只检测到HOKV阳性;其中5只RNA阳性但抗体阴性(急性感染),18只RNA阳性且抗体滴度高、亲和力强(持续感染);病毒RNA和抗原在肺、肾、脾中检出;在2024次捕获的样本中,所有口腔拭子、6份尿液中有3份、6份粪便中有4份均分离到有感染性的病毒。
analysis_zh.contribution: 研究表明HOKV在其自然宿主中可建立急性和持续感染,且不引起显著病理变化,显示出多种排病毒途径,唾液可能是主要来源,并深化了对汉坦病毒生态与维持机制的认识,为跨种传播风险评估提供依据。
analysis_zh.limitations: 未在所提供证据中报告。

papers.title: A historical cross-border Andes virus lineage reveals the origin of a cruise ship hantavirus pulmonary syndrome outbreak
papers.abstract: Andes virus (ANDV) caused a multi-country outbreak of hantavirus pulmonary syndrome among passengers and crew of a cruise ship in 2026. To investigate the origin and evolutionary history of the virus responsible for the outbreak, we analyzed complete ANDV small (S), medium (M), and large (L) genome segment sequences from Chile alongside outbreak-associated and publicly available genome sequences. Across all three segment-specific phylogenies, the outbreak virus clustered within an ANDV Clade III cluster spanning southern Chile and northern Patagonia in Argentina and were most closely related to a human-derived ANDV (p1236) collected in Los Rios Region of Chile in 2012, representing the closest known historical relative of the outbreak-associated virus. Phylogeographic analysis showed that the genetically distinct ANDV Clade V lineage circulating in central Chile was not closely related to the cruise ship outbreak-associated genomes, thereby reducing the likelihood that the index cases acquired infection through zoonotic spillover while traveling through the Maule Region. These findings trace the geographic origin of the outbreak-associated virus to a defined corridor, the Hua Hum Pass, a cross-border zone connecting Neuquen Province in Argentina with the Los Rios and La Araucania Regions of Chile.

Phylogeographic analysis showed that the genetically distinct ANDV Clade V lineage circulating in central Chile was not closely related to the cruise ship outbreak-associated genomes, thereby reducing the likelihood that the index cases acquired infection through zoonotic spillover while traveling through the Maule Region. These findings trace the geographic origin of the outbreak-associated virus to a defined corridor, the Hua Hum Pass, a cross-border zone connecting Neuquen Province in Argentina with the Los Rios and La Araucania Regions of Chile.

Kuhn7†, Carla M. Bellomo8, Andreas Schüller2,9, Raúl Araya- 5 Secchi10, International Genomics Consortium Investigating the M/V Hondius Outbreak11, 6 Daniel M. Cisterna12, Luciana Fernandez-Bettelli7, Santiago Palacios-Aliggi7, Valeria P. 7 Martínez8, Marcela Ferrés1,13,14, Piet Maes15*, Gustavo Palacios7,16*, Nicole D. Tischler5,17* 8 & Jenniffer Angulo1,2* 9 1Departamento de Enfermedades Infecciosas e Inmunología Pediátricas, Escuela de 10 Medicina, Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile, Santiago, Chile 11 2Facultad de Ciencias Biológicas, Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile, Santiago, Chile 12 3Escuela de Medicina Veterinaria, Facultad de Medicina, Pontificia Universidad Católica 13 de Chile, Santiago, Chile 14 4Facultad de Agronomía y Sistemas Naturales, Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile, 15 Santiago, Chile 16 5Laboratorio de Virología Molecular, Centro Ciencia & Vida, Fundación Ciencia & Vida, , 17 Santiago, Chile 18 6Doctorado en Biotecnología y Bioemprendimiento, Facultad de Medicina, Universidad 19 San Sebastián, Santiago, Chile 20 7Department of Microbiology, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, New York, NY, 21 USA 22 8Laboratorio Nacional de Referencia de Hantavirus, Instituto Nacional de Enfermedades 23 Infecciosas, Administración Nacional de Laboratorios e Institutos de Salud Dr. Carlos G. 24 Malbrán; Buenos Aires, Argentina 25 9Institute for Biological and Medical Engineering, Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile, 26 Santiago, Chile 27 10Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad San Sebastián, Valdivia, Chile and Centro de 28 Estudios Científicos, Valdivia, Chile 29 11The full list of the authors and affiliations of this consortium can be found in the 30 Supplementary Material 31 12 Instituto Nacional de Enfermedades Infecciosas, Administración Nacional de 32 Laboratorios e Institutos de Salud Dr. Carlos G. Malbrán; Buenos Aires, Argentina 33 13Laboratorio de Infectología y Virología Molecular, Red Salud UC-Christus, Santiago, 34 Chile 35 14SENTINET, Santiago, Chile 36 15European Plotkin Institute for Vaccinology, Université Libre de Bruxelles, Brussels, 37 Belgium 38 16Global Health Emerging Pathogens Institute, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, 39 New York, NY, USA 40 17Escuela de Bioquímica, Facultad de Ciencias, Universidad San Sebastián, Santiago, Chile 41 *e-mails: jangulc@uc.cl; ntischler@cienciavida.org; gustavo.palacios@mssm.edu; 42 piet.maes@ulb.be 43 †These authors contributed equally to this work. 44 Abstract 45 Andes virus (ANDV) caused a multi-country outbreak of hantavirus pulmonary syndrome 46 among passengers and crew of a cruise ship in 2026. To investigate the origin and 47 evolutionary history of the virus responsible for the outbreak, we analyzed complete 48 ANDV small (S), medium (M), and large (L) genome segment sequences from Chile 49 alongside outbreak-associated and publicly available genome sequences. Across all three 50 segment-specific phylogenies, the outbreak virus clustered within an ANDV Clade III 51 cluster spanning southern Chile and northern Patagonia in Argentina and were most closely 52 related to a human-derived ANDV (p1236) collected in Los Ríos Region of Chile in 2012, 53 representing the closest known historical relative of the outbreak-associated virus. 54 Phylogeographic analysis showed that the genetically distinct ANDV Clade V lineage 55 circulating in central Chile was not closely related to the cruise ship outbreak-associated 56 genomes, thereby reducing the likelihood that the index cases acquired infection through 57 zoonotic spillover while traveling through the Maule Region. These findings trace the 58 geographic origin of the outbreak-associated virus to a defined corridor, the Hua Hum Pass, 59 a cross-border zone connecting Neuquén Province in Argentina with the Los Ríos and La 60 Araucanía Regions of Chile. 61 Introduction 62 The April–May 2026 outbreak of hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HPS) aboard the Dutch- 63 flagged M/V Hondius cruise ship1 represented an unusual international displacement of an 64 orthohantavirus, apparently initiated by zoonotic spillover and followed by human-to- 65 human transmission during the ship voyage. Recent genomic and epidemiological 66 investigations characterized the outbreak variant and reconstructed key elements of the 67 travel history of the presumed index cases1. However, the precise geographic site and 68 circumstances of the initial zoonotic spillover remain unresolved. 69 Andes virus (ANDV) is a rodent-borne orthohantavirus in subfamily Mammantavirinae of 70 family Hantaviridae. It is classified within the species Orthohantavirus andesense together 71 with three closely related “ANDV-like” viruses, Castelo dos Sonhos virus (CASV), 72 Lechiguanas virus (LECV), and Orán virus (ORNV). Like all orthohantaviruses, ANDV 73 has a trisegmented negative-sense RNA genome2. 74 Genomic analysis identified the cruise ship outbreak agent as ANDV and showed that the 75 outbreak-associated genome sequences were most closely related to ANDV genome 76 sequences causing HPS cases in Province of Neuquén, Argentina3,4. In particular, the cruise 77 ship outbreak virus genome sequence clustered with sequences from HPS cases in Villa 78 Meliquina, San Martín de los Andes, and Villa Traful, documented between 2017 and 79 20264. Pairwise comparisons showed approximately 98.7% nucleotide identity between the 80 cruise-associated virus sequence and the closest Province of Neuquén small (S), medium 81 (M), and large (L) genome segment sequences4. The cruise variant reference sequence, 82 ANDV-Switzerland-Hu-3337-2026, differs from the NRC4/2018 Province of Neuquén 83 reference sequence by 23 single nucleotide variants (SNVs) in the S, 47 in the M, and 84 in 84 the L segment. Most mutations were synonymous; nonsynonymous mutations were 85 predicted to result in V70I and K144R changes in the large (L) protein (encoded by the L 86 segment), and T193A, T516I, and A994T changes in glycoprotein precursor (GPC; 87 encoded by the M segment)3,5. These genome sequences from Province of Neuquén were 88 interpreted3,4 as the closest sampled human representatives of a broader ANDV population 89 circulating across northern Patagonia in Argentina and southern Chile. However, they are 90 not sufficiently closely related to be considered direct ancestors of the cruise ship outbreak- 91 associated virus. 92 Closely related rodent-derived sequences were recently made available6. In a pairwise 93 comparison against the Swiss cruise-ship reference genome sequence 94 ANDV/Switzerland/Hu-3337/2026 (PZ385161.1, PZ385162.1, and PZ385163.1)7, the 95 closest rodent-derived ANDV sequence from Chile in the available set, R175, still differs 96 by 68 unambiguous nucleotide substitutions across the three segments: 36 SNVs in L, 23 in 97 M, and 9 in S. This level of divergence is still substantially greater than that reported by 98 Kim et al.8, who used active targeted surveillance to identify the geographical site of 99 Hantaan virus emergence. In their study, paired human and rodent Hantaan virus sequences 100 differed by only 8–30 nucleotide substitutions across the complete trisegmented genome, 101 depending on the human–rodent pair. Thus, the currently available rodent sequences from 102 Chile should be interpreted as evidence of broader unsampled regional diversity rather than 103 as the likely immediate source. 104 Our epidemiological reconstruction showed that the presumed index cases, Cases 1 and 2, 105 travelled extensively by motorhome through Argentina, Uruguay and Chile before 106 embarking on the cruise in Ushuaia, Province of Tierra del Fuego, Antarctica and South 107 Atlantic Islands, Argentina, on 1 April 2026. This work will focus on their travel through 108 Chile. They entered Chile’s Aysén Region on 9 January, returning to Argentina via the 109 Icalma Pass on 31 January. These regions are known for the circulation of ANDV and the 110 presence of long-tailed colilargos (Oligoryzomys longicaudatus (Bennett, 1832)), ANDV’s 111 main spillover rodent reservoirs9. On 12 February, Cases 1 and 2 moved via the Pehuenche 112 Pass to Maule Region in Chile, and re-entered Argentina four days later via the same pass 113 (Figure 3). 114 Recently generated complete genome sequence data from human-derived ANDV in Chile, 115 spanning a period between 2011–202410, now provides an opportunity to evaluate this 116 possibility in a higher-resolution phylogeographic framework. That dataset defines 117 geographically structured ANDV diversity in Chile, including a new northern/central 118 lineage (ANDV Chi-North, from now on referred to as ANDV Clade V). In the present 119 study, we expanded this framework by incorporating additional 2025–2026 sequences from 120 Chile together with HPS outbreak-associated ANDV genomes. By comparing the cruise 121 ship outbreak variant sequence with this expanded dataset and by mapping those 122 relationships against the reconstructed travel itinerary of the index cases, we evaluate 123 whether the initial zoonotic ANDV spillover could have occurred in Chile or in an 124 unsampled cross-border extension of the northern Patagonia from Argentina. 125 Results 126 Cruise ship outbreak-associated Andes virus genome sequences cluster with those of 127 Andes viruses circulating in the Hua Hum Pass corridor 128 To better understand the ancestry of the cruise ship-associated ANDV, we leveraged three 129 sequence datasets populated exclusively with complete genome sequences comprising the 130 ANDV S, M, and L segments: cruise ship outbreak-associated ANDV, Argentina-endemic 131 ANDV, and Chile-endemic ANDV genome sequences. This complete genome sequence 132 approach was adopted to minimize biases arising from segment-specific phylogenetic 133 discordance and to enable direct comparison across the S, M, and L segments. Maximum- 134 likelihood phylogenetic analyses placed each cruise ship outbreak-associated genome 135 segment sequence within previously described ANDV diversity, clustering with sequences 136 belonging to ANDV Clade III2, in a distinct cluster, here designated “Hua Hum Pass 137 corridor (HHPC)”, composed predominantly of historical human-derived ANDV genome 138 sequences from La Araucanía and Los Ríos Regions in Chile together with related northern 139 Patagonian sequences from Argentina (Fig. 1). 140 Within the S segment phylogeny (Fig. 1a), cruise ship outbreak-associated genome 141 sequences clustered with sequences derived from Chile p1236 (Los Ríos Region, 2012), 142 p931 (Los Ríos Region, 2020), p1242 (Los Ríos Region, 2013), p1239 (Los Ríos Region, 143 2013), p132D (Los Ríos Region, 2026), and p1059 (La Araucanía Region, 2023), together 144 with Argentinian Province of Neuquén genome sequences NRC-3/18 and NRC-4/18. 145 Similar clustering patterns were observed independently in the M (Fig. 1b) and L (Fig. 1c) 146 segment phylogeny. 147 Within the HHPC cluster, p1236 emerged as the closest sampled human-derived relative of 148 the cruise ship outbreak-associated viruses across all three genome segments. The patient 149 was a 7-year-old male from the Lanco commune (Los Ríos Region, southern Chile) who 150 experienced symptom onset on November 7, 2012. Epidemiologically, his infection was an 151 isolated incident linked to rural exposure, with no associated cluster or secondary cases. He 152 sought treatment at the hospital in Panguipulli, where his condition deteriorated into a 153 severe presentation of HPS. Because ECMO was unavailable at that specific facility, his 154 critical respiratory failure was managed with invasive mechanical ventilation. This life- 155 saving intervention firmly aligns his illness with the study's "severe" clinical classification. 156 Despite the critical, life-threatening nature of the disease, the child successfully survived 157 and was discharged on November 22, 2012. Although the complete L segment could not be 158 recovered for p1236, the available partial L sequence contained two unresolved regions at 159 positions 4008–5185 and 5461–6562. Nevertheless, the available L-segment regions 160 supported the same phylogenetic placement observed for the S and M segments. In each 161 segment-specific phylogeny, p1236 is positioned more closely to the cruise ship outbreak 162 genome sequences than NRC-4/2018, which had previously represented the closest 163 available complete human-derived comparator3,4. Direct comparison between p1236 and 164 the Swiss outbreak-associated case revealed 18 nucleotide differences in the S segment, 165 with no nonsynonymous substitutions; 28 nucleotide differences in the M segment, 166 including two nonsynonymous substitutions; and 78 nucleotide differences across the 167 4,535-nt available portion of the L segment, including three nonsynonymous substitutions. 168 Although overall similarity levels remained comparable, these findings substantially refine 169 the genomic context of the cruise ship outbreak-associated variant by linking it to a 170 historical human-derived ANDV detected in southern Chile rather than exclusively to 171 northern Patagonian genome sequences from Argentina. 172 Bayesian phylogenetic analyses recovered highly consistent maximum clade credibility 173 (MCC) trees, corroborating the phylogenetic placement inferred by maximum-likelihood 174 analyses (Supplementary Figure S1). 175 Motivated by published evidence suggesting potential reassortment including the 176 phylogenetic placement of the publicly available rodent-derived genome R600 from the 177 Los Ríos Region of Chile (2013), which clustered with the outbreak-associated genomes in 178 the L-segment phylogeny but not in the S and M phylogenies7, we performed a 179 comprehensive reassortment analysis across our complete dataset. This analysis detected no 180 evidence of reassortment among Clade III genomes (Supplementary Figure S2), 181 supporting the interpretation that the HHPC cluster represents a single evolutionary lineage. 182 Several additional human-associated genome sequences composing the HHPC cluster were 183 obtained from HPS cases identified since 2012 in southern Chile. These cases included 184 sporadic infections, epidemiologically linked clusters, and a range of clinical outcomes 185 (Supplementary Table S1). Together, these findings indicate that the HHPC cluster 186 represents a long-standing component of ANDV diversity in southern Chile and northern 187 Patagonia in Argentina. 188 The Hua Hum Pass corridor Andes virus cluster is defined by limited, largely neutral 189 molecular signatures within Andes virus Clade III diversity 190 No major genomic insertions, deletions, or segment-specific genomic features distinguished 191 HHPC cluster genome sequences from those of other ANDV Clade III members. 192 Comparison of deduced amino acid variation identified a limited number of 193 phylogenetically informative substitutions. Consistent with previous analyses10, ANDV 194 Clade III was characterized by substitutions including GPC I114V, A193T and V1127I. 195 Within this genetic background, genomes composing the HHPC cluster consistently 196 encoded GPC T1055, whereas the remaining analyzed ANDV Clade III genomes encoded 197 GPC A1055. Additional cluster-enriched changes included nucleoprotein (S segment) 198 substitution L7I and L protein (L segment) R144K and A541V. Together, these 199 substitutions define a molecular signature that distinguishes the HHPC cluster within the 200 broader diversity of ANDV Clade III (Supplementary Table S2). A comprehensive 201 comparison of all deduced amino acid substitutions identified among HHPC cluster 202 genome sequences relative to the five recognized ANDV clades (Clades I–V) and 203 representative orthohantaviruses is provided in Supplementary Table S3. 204 Previously proposed amino acid markers associated with person-to-person transmission in 205 Argentinian HPS outbreaks11 were not consistently encoded in HHPC cluster genome 206 sequences. The only substitution unique to the cruise ship outbreak lineage was GPC 207 T516I, which remained exclusive after inclusion of the expanded genomic dataset from 208 Chile. In the absence of structural or comparative evidence supporting an effect on 209 glycoprotein stability or function, T516I is best interpreted as a molecular marker of the 210 common-source outbreak, potentially reflecting stochastic drift in the local reservoir 211 population. Its phenotypic relevance will require direct functional testing in virion-like 212 particle (VLP) or pseudotype systems. 213 Structural mapping of HHPC cluster-associated SNV-encoded amino acid residue changes 214 onto GPC-encoded GN/Gc showed that three of the five substitutions are located in surface- 215 exposed regions of the spikes, whereas the remaining two are located within the GN 216 endodomain and GC transmembrane (Supplementary Figure S3). Although the surface- 217 exposed substitutions could be compatible with immune-driven selection, none overlapped 218 with previously reported neutralizing antibody epitopes or escape variants. Similarly, the 219 three L protein-associated substitutions mapped outside of the known catalytic sites of this 220 multifunctional protein12 (Supplementary Figure S4). The potential structural impact was 221 further analyzed with deep learning-based tools, which indicated only minor changes in 222 Gibbs free energy (ΔΔG) values, suggesting that these variants are unlikely to substantially 223 affect protein stability or quaternary assembly. Together, the conservative physicochemical 224 nature of the residue substitutions and pervasive purifying selection across ANDV proteins 225 (dN/dS <1) sup…

analysis.limitations: not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus (ANDV) caused a multi‑country outbreak of hantavirus pulmonary syndrome among passengers and crew of a cruise ship in 2026. Methods: The study analyzed complete ANDV small (S), medium (M), and large (L) genome segment sequences from Chile alongside outbreak‑associated and publicly available genome sequences. Results: Across all three segment‑specific phylogenies, the outbreak virus clustered within an ANDV Clade III spanning southern Chile and northern Patagonia in Argentina and was most closely related to a human‑derived ANDV (p1236) collected in Los Ríos Region of Chile in 2012. Contribution: Phylogeographic analysis showed that the genetically distinct ANDV Clade V lineage circulating in central Chile was not closely related to the cruise ship outbreak‑associated genomes, reducing the likelihood that the index cases acquired infection through zoonotic spillover while traveling through the Maule Region. Limitations: not reported in the supplied evidence.

papers.title_zh: 历史性跨境 安第斯病毒 系统揭示了邮轮 汉坦病毒肺综合征 爆发的起源

analysis_zh.background: 安第斯病毒(ANDV)在2026年导致一次跨国汉坦病毒肺综合征暴发,感染对象为一艘邮轮的乘客和船员。
analysis_zh.methods: 本研究分析了来自智利的完整ANDV小(S)段、中(M)段和大(L)段基因组序列,并与暴发相关及公开可得的基因组序列进行比较。
analysis_zh.results: 在全部三段特异的系统发育树中,暴发病毒聚类于覆盖智利南部和阿根廷北部巴塔哥尼亚的ANDV Clade III系,并与2012年在智利洛斯里奥斯地区采集的人源ANDV(p1236)最为接近。
analysis_zh.contribution: 系统地理分析显示,流行于智利中部的基因上独特的ANDV Clade V血统与邮轮暴发相关基因组关系不密切,降低了指数病例在穿越Maule Region地区旅行时通过动物源溢出感染的可能性。
analysis_zh.limitations: 未在所提供证据中报告。

analysis.background: The scoping review is being undertaken to map therapeutic intervention strategies for hantavirus infection, a disease with limited treatment options (A1).
analysis.main_directions: The protocol intends to identify and categorize pharmacologic, supportive, and other therapeutic approaches reported for hantavirus infection (A1).
analysis.current_state: No synthesized evidence exists yet; the protocol outlines a systematic process to collate available data (A1).
analysis.gaps: There is currently no comprehensive synthesis of hantavirus therapeutic studies, and the literature base is unclear (A1).
analysis.future_research: Completion of the scoping review, followed by targeted systematic reviews and clinical trials to evaluate promising interventions, is recommended (A1).

analysis.summary_en: Background: The scoping review aims to map therapeutic strategies for hantavirus infection, a disease with limited treatments. Main directions: It will catalogue pharmacologic, supportive, and other interventions. Current state: No synthesis exists yet; the protocol defines a systematic mapping process. Gaps: A comprehensive synthesis of hantavirus therapeutic evidence is lacking. Future research: Finish the scoping review and pursue systematic reviews and clinical trials of identified promising therapies.

papers.title: Hantavirus and Middle East Public Health Challenges: A Subject Review
papers.abstract: Hantaviruses are emerging zoonotic viruses belonging to the family Hantaviridae, which are typically found in rodent reservoirs and transmitted to humans through contact with contaminated saliva, aerosolized particles or excreta. There are two major clinical syndromes associated with human infections: hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) — mostly reported from Europe and Asia, and hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS) — mostly reported from the Americas. The Middle East has a relatively low incidence of hantavirus disease, but recent epidemiological studies have shown that the virus is circulating in several countries, including Türkiye and Iran. The emergence of hantavirus infections in this area has become a growing concern as the population increases, climate variations occur, the rodents' ecology is changing, the agriculture spreads and the surveillance of this situation is improving. There is limited surveillance, underdiagnosis, and a large clinical overlap with other febrile illnesses all of which are important challenges in quantifying the regional burden of disease. This review aims at summarizing the state of knowledge about the epidemiology of the hantavirus in the Middle East, focusing on geographical distribution, environmental factors, rodent hosts, epidemiology, clinical features, diagnostic methods and public health preparedness. Current data suggest that while human infections are rare, ecological conditions which could sustain hantavirus are present in several Middle Eastern countries, and therefore increased surveillance and research into interdisciplinary and One Health initiatives are needed. Creating closer ties between the medical, veterinary and environmental communities is crucial for early outbreak detection and prevention in the future.
papers.journal: European Journal of Ecology, Biology and Agriculture

analysis.background: Hantaviruses are emerging zoonotic viruses transmitted from rodent reservoirs via saliva, aerosols, or excreta. (A1)
analysis.main_directions: The review covers geographic distribution, environmental drivers, rodent hosts, epidemiology, clinical presentation, diagnostics, and public‑health preparedness in the Middle East. (A6)
analysis.current_state: Recent studies show low but detectable hantavirus circulation in countries such as Türkiye and Iran, with human cases remaining rare. (A3)
analysis.gaps: Surveillance is limited, cases are underdiagnosed, and clinical features overlap with other febrile illnesses, hindering accurate burden estimation. (A5)
analysis.future_research: Expanded surveillance, interdisciplinary One‑Health initiatives, and stronger collaboration among medical, veterinary, and environmental sectors are recommended. (A7)

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses are emerging zoonotic viruses transmitted from rodent reservoirs via saliva, aerosols, or excreta. Main directions: The review covers geographic distribution, environmental drivers, rodent hosts, epidemiology, clinical presentation, diagnostics, and public‑health preparedness in the Middle East. Current state: Recent studies show low but detectable hantavirus circulation in Türkiye and Iran, with human cases remaining rare. Gaps: Surveillance is limited, underdiagnosis and clinical overlap with other febrile illnesses impede burden estimation. Future research: Expanded surveillance, One‑Health initiatives, and tighter medical‑veterinary‑environmental collaboration are needed.

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒 和中东公共卫生挑战:主题回顾

analysis_zh.background: 汉坦病毒es 是新出现的人畜共患病毒,通过啮齿动物宿主的唾液、气溶胶或排泄物传播。(A1)
analysis_zh.main_directions: 本综述涵盖中东地区的地理分布、环境驱动因素、啮齿动物宿主、流行病学、临床表现、诊断以及公共卫生应急准备。(A6)
analysis_zh.current_state: 近期研究显示,在土耳其和伊朗等国,汉坦病毒 的循环水平虽低但可检测,且人类病例仍然罕见。(A3)
analysis_zh.gaps: 监测有限,病例漏诊,临床表现与其他发热性疾病重叠,阻碍了对负担的准确估计。(A5)
analysis_zh.future_research: 建议扩大监测,开展跨学科 One‐Health 项目,并加强医学、兽医和环境部门之间的协作。(A7)

papers.summary_zh: 背景：汉坦病毒es 是新出现的人畜共患病毒,通过啮齿动物宿主的唾液、气溶胶或排泄物传播。(A1) 主要方向：本综述涵盖中东地区的地理分布、环境驱动因素、啮齿动物宿主、流行病学、临床表现、诊断以及公共卫生应急准备。(A6) 研究现状：近期研究显示,在土耳其和伊朗等国,汉坦病毒 的循环水平虽低但可检测,且人类病例仍然罕见。(A3) 不足：监测有限,病例漏诊,临床表现与其他发热性疾病重叠,阻碍了对负担的准确估计。(A5) 后续研究：建议扩大监测,开展跨学科 One‐Health 项目,并加强医学、兽医和环境部门之间的协作。(A7)

papers.title: Hantavirus preparedness among undergraduate nursing students in the United Arab Emirates: a cross-sectional study of knowledge, risk perception and preventive practice.
papers.abstract: BACKGROUND: Hantavirus infection is uncommon in many settings but can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease. Nursing students require disease-specific preparedness for exposure-history taking, safe prevention counselling and infection prevention during community and clinical learning. This study examined undergraduate nursing students' knowledge, risk perception and preventive practice toward hantavirus and identified associated factors. METHODS: A descriptive analytical cross-sectional survey was conducted among 236 undergraduate nursing students in one nursing program in Ras Al Khaimah, the United Arab Emirates during the 2025-2026 academic year. Data was collected using the researcher-developed Hantavirus Knowledge, Attitudes and Preventive Practices Questionnaire for Nursing Students (HKAPP-NS). Descriptive statistics, Cronbach's alpha, Pearson correlations and multivariable linear regression with HC3 robust standard errors were used. RESULTS: Knowledge was low among the 236 complete-case respondents (mean 8.13/20, SD 5.84), with 59.0% classified as having poor knowledge. Attitude was moderate (mean 33.34/50, SD 7.78), and overall practice was higher (mean 42.98/64, SD 15.83). Internal consistency was strong across domains (alpha 0.886-0.967). Attitude correlated with overall practice (r = 0.569, p < 0.001), whereas knowledge did not (r = 0.101, p = 0.120). Previous hantavirus teaching, infection prevention and control training and environmental exposure predicted higher knowledge. Attitude was the strongest independent predictor of overall practice. CONCLUSIONS: Students reported comparatively frequent precautionary practice but had important disease-specific knowledge gaps, particularly regarding safe rodent-contamination cleanup, routine vaccine availability, specific antiviral treatment and person-to-person transmission. The finding that attitude, rather than knowledge, independently predicted practice suggests that scenario-based One Health education should combine factual teaching with risk appraisal, self-efficacy building, exposure-history taking and prevention counselling to strengthen future nurses' preparedness for rodent-borne zoonotic infections.

Results Knowledge was low among the 236 complete-case respondents (mean 8.13/20, SD 5.84), with 59.0% classified as having poor knowledge. Attitude was moderate (mean 33.34/50, SD 7.78), and overall practice was higher (mean 42.98/64, SD 15.83). Internal consistency was strong across domains (alpha 0.886–0.967). Background Hantaviruses are zoonotic viruses maintained in rodent reservoirs and are clinically important because infection, although uncommon in many settings, can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease. Human infection most often follows inhalation of aerosolised particles from infected rodent urine, droppings, saliva or nesting material, and exposure can also occur when contaminated material contacts broken skin or mucous membranes. Hantavirus ecology, epidemiology and clinical presentations vary globally, with manifestations including hantavirus pulmonary syndrome and haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome. Recent international attention to Andes virus has reinforced the need for health professionals to understand both the usual rodent-borne pathway and the rare person-to-person exception. In May 2026, WHO and CDC reported a multi-country Andes-virus-associated hantavirus cluster linked to cruise ship travel. Andes virus is the only hantavirus currently known to spread from person to person, usually through close contact with a symptomatic person, whereas most hantaviruses are not commonly transmitted between people. This distinction has direct implications for exposure-history taking, risk communication, triage, isolation decisions and infection prevention education. Prevention is largely behavioural and environmental. Current guidance emphasises reducing contact between people and rodents, sealing rodent entry points, storing food securely, avoiding dry sweeping or vacuuming of rodent-contaminated materials, dampening contaminated areas before cleaning and applying appropriate infection prevention and control (IPC) measures in health-care settings. Therefore, preparedness depends not only on generic IPC knowledge but also on disease-specific understanding of rodent reservoirs, exposure routes, safe cleanup and practical risk-reduction messages. Undergraduate nursing students are a relevant population for hantavirus preparedness. During community health learning, home visits, environmental health activities, primary care experiences and clinical placements, students may encounter patients with non-specific febrile illness, respiratory symptoms, household rodent contamination or environmental exposure histories. They therefore need sufficient understanding to ask about rodent exposure, recognise when escalation is needed, provide safe prevention advice and practise IPC under supervision. These competencies require a One Health approach rather than a purely biomedical or classroom-based IPC approach, because safe nursing responses to hantavirus depend on recognising connections between human symptoms, rodent reservoirs, household and environmental contamination, cleaning practices and community risk communication. Embedding One Health in nursing curricula can help students translate reservoir ecology and environmental exposure information into exposure-history taking, patient education, escalation decisions and IPC behaviours at the point of care. Knowledge-attitude-practice surveys can identify modifiable gaps in factual knowledge, risk perception and self-reported behaviours, although results should be interpreted with awareness of contextual and social desirability limitations. Evidence from nursing education research suggests that knowledge and attitudes can shape students’ infectious disease management behaviours. However, disease-specific evidence on nursing students’ hantavirus preparedness remains limited. This gap matters because general IPC instruction may not correct practical misconceptions about safe rodent-contamination cleanup, vaccine availability, specific antiviral treatment or overgeneralised assumptions about person-to-person transmission. The aim of this study was to examine undergraduate nursing students’ knowledge, attitudes/risk perception, clinical practices and preventive practices toward hantavirus and to identify associated factors. The objectives were to describe students’ sociodemographic and exposure characteristics; estimate levels of knowledge, attitude and practice; assess internal consistency of questionnaire domains; examine correlations among knowledge, attitude and practice; and identify independent predictors of knowledge, attitude and overall practice. Methods Study design and reporting This was a descriptive analytical cross-sectional study. The manuscript was prepared in accordance with the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) recommendations for cross-sectional studies. Setting, participants and recruitment The study was conducted among undergraduate nursing students enrolled in a nursing program at RAK Medical and Health Sciences University, Ras Al Khaimah, United Arab Emirates during the 2025–2026 academic year after ethical approval had been obtained. Eligible participants were undergraduate nursing students who provided informed consent and completed the structured questionnaire. Students who declined participation or submitted questionnaires with no valid domain responses were excluded from the study. A non-probability convenience sampling strategy was used. All eligible undergraduate nursing students enrolled in the program were approached through program-level recruitment procedures and invited to participate voluntarily. Recruitment was conducted across all years of study to maximize coverage of the student cohort. A total of 500 eligible students were invited, and 236 submitted questionnaires with valid demographic, exposure and domain-score data and were included in the complete-case analyses. Complete data were therefore available for 47.2% of the invited cohort. The recruitment records captured the number invited and the number with analyzable complete data, but did not permit reliable separation of nonresponse, refusal and partial completion among the remaining 264 invited students. Where a submitted questionnaire lacked information required to calculate demographic, exposure or one or more domain scores, it was excluded from the complete-case analysis. No item-level imputation was performed. Study size Study-size adequacy was assessed for multivariable regression analyses because identifying predictors of knowledge, attitude and practice was a main analytic objective. Using G*Power 3.1 for linear multiple regression (fixed model, R2 deviation from zero), with a medium effect size (f2 = 0.15), alpha = 0.05, power = 0.80 and 9 predictors in the largest model, the minimum required sample was 114 students. Allowing for 20% incomplete data gave a minimum target of 143 students. The achieved complete-case sample of 236 exceeded this requirement and Green’s regression rule of thumb (N > = 50 + 8 m; with 9 predictors, minimum N = 122). Instrument and variables Data were collected using the Hantavirus Knowledge, Attitudes and Preventive Practices Questionnaire for Nursing Students (HKAPP-NS). The HKAPP-NS, including the questionnaire items and scoring scales, was developed by the study researchers in English specifically for this study and has not been published elsewhere. Item content was informed by current WHO and CDC hantavirus guidance and KAP survey methodology. The investigator team internally reviewed draft items for clarity, relevance and consistency with current guidance and undergraduate nursing competencies. No formal face-validity or content-validity assessment by an independent expert panel was undertaken before deployment, and neither item-level nor scale-level Content Validity Indexing was calculated. Accordingly, the HKAPP-NS should be interpreted as a researcher-developed, study-specific instrument with preliminary internal-consistency evidence, rather than as a fully validated scale. The questionnaire included sociodemographic and exposure variables, a 20-item knowledge section, a 10-item attitude/risk-perception section, an 8-item clinical-practice section and an 8-item preventive-practice section. Sociodemographic and exposure variables included age, sex, year of study, residence, living arrangement, previous zoonotic disease teaching, previous hantavirus teaching, IPC clinical-placement training, reported rodent or rodent-dropping exposure and environmental exposure activities. Scoring and data management Knowledge responses were scored 1 for correct and 0 for incorrect or ‘do not know’. The correct answer was true for items 1, 2, 4–14 and 18–20, and false for items 3, 15, 16 and 17. Knowledge was categorised as poor (0–9), moderate (10–15) or good (16–20), corresponding to < 50%, 50–75% and > 75% of the total score. Attitude items used a 1–5 Likert scale; the anxiety item (item 8) was reverse scored so that higher scores reflected more favorable attitudes/risk perception. Clinical-practice and preventive-practice items used a 0–4 frequency scale, with higher scores indicating more frequent recommended practice. The overall practice score was calculated by summing clinical and preventive practice scores. Overall practice was categorized as low (0–31), moderate (32–47) or high (48–64), corresponding to < 50%, 50–75% and > 75% of the total score. For regression modelling, rodent/droppings exposure was analyzed as a dichotomous indicator of any report of seeing rodents or droppings in the past 12 months, and environmental exposure represented any listed exposure activity. Complete cases were defined as respondents with valid demographic, exposure and domain responses sufficient to calculate all study scores. Because recruitment records did not retain separate disposition codes for nonresponse, refusal and partial completion, the 264 invited students who did not contribute complete data could not be disaggregated further. Demographic, exposure, domain-score, correlation and regression analyses used the 236 complete cases; no item-level imputation was performed. Sex and gender considerations Sex was self-reported using questionnaire response categories. The study did not collect gender identity data. Because the study was not powered for sex-stratified analyses and the male subgroup was smaller than the female subgroup, sex was used as an adjustment variable rather than as the basis for subgroup interpretation. Statistical analysis Analyses were conducted using SPSS 27. Continuous variables were summarized using mean, standard deviation (SD), median, interquartile range (IQR) and range. Categorical variables were summarized using frequencies and percentages. Internal consistency was assessed using Cronbach’s alpha; very high alpha values were interpreted as evidence of internal consistency but also as possible item overlap. Pearson correlation coefficients assessed relationships among knowledge, attitude and overall practice scores. Multivariable linear regression models with HC3 robust standard errors were fitted for knowledge, attitude and overall practice scores. Model building used an a priori, conceptually guided KAP framework; no automated stepwise selection was used. The largest knowledge model included age, sex, year of study, urban residence, previous zoonotic disease teaching, previous hantavirus teaching, IPC training, rodent/droppings exposure and any environmental exposure. The attitude model was specified to test demographic, education/exposure and cognitive factors linked to risk perception: sex, previous zoonotic disease teaching, any environmental exposure and knowledge score. The overall-practice model was specified to test the environmental cue and KAP-domain predictors most directly linked to behavior: rodent/droppings exposure, attitude score and knowledge score. Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05. Regression coefficients are unstandardized beta coefficients. Use of AI-assisted tools in manuscript preparation During manuscript preparation, the authors used OpenAI ChatGPT to support language editing, journal formatting and organization of the submission package. The tool was not used to generate study data or conduct statistical analyses. The authors reviewed and edited the content and take full responsibility for the submitted manuscript. Ethical considerations Ethical approval was obtained from the Human Ethics Committee, RAK Medical and Health Sciences University (RAKMHSU-HEC; approval number RAKMHSU/RES/HEC/2025-26/114; dated 2 April 2026). All procedures involving human participants were performed in accordance with relevant institutional, national and international guidelines and regulations, including the Declaration of Helsinki. Participants provided informed consent before completing the questionnaire. Participation was voluntary, and responses were analyzed anonymously or confidentially according to institutional requirements. Results Participant characteristics Of the 236 complete-case respondents, the mean age was 21.45 years (SD 2.19; range 17–30). Most respondents were female, lived in urban areas and lived in family homes. Most had not received previous zoonotic disease teaching or previous hantavirus teaching, and a minority reported completing IPC clinical-placement training. A subset reported seeing rodents or rodent droppings in the past 12 months or participating in environmental exposure activities such as cleaning dusty closed storage, farm or field visits, camping or hiking, or contact with pet rodents (Table 1). Domain-score, correlation and regression analyses used 236 complete cases Domain scores and reliability Knowledge was low overall among the 236 complete-case respondents. The mean knowledge score was 8.13 out of 20 (SD 5.84); 59.0% were categorized as having poor knowledge, 19.2% had moderate knowledge and 21.8% had good knowledge. Attitude was moderate overall (mean 33.34 out of 50, SD 7.78). Overall practice had a mean of 42.98 out of 64 (SD 15.83); 23.5% of students had low practice, 32.0% had moderate practice and 44.5% had high practice.
