full_text_sections.full_text: Hantavirus preparedness among undergraduate nursing students in the United Arab Emirates: a cross-sectional study of knowledge, risk perception and preventive practice Background Hantavirus infection is uncommon in many settings but can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease. Nursing students require disease-specific preparedness for exposure-history taking, safe prevention counselling and infection prevention during community and clinical learning. This study examined undergraduate nursing students’ knowledge, risk perception and preventive practice toward hantavirus and identified associated factors. Methods A descriptive analytical cross-sectional survey was conducted among 236 undergraduate nursing students in one nursing program in Ras Al Khaimah, the United Arab Emirates during the 2025–2026 academic year. Data was collected using the researcher-developed Hantavirus Knowledge, Attitudes and Preventive Practices Questionnaire for Nursing Students (HKAPP-NS). Descriptive statistics, Cronbach’s alpha, Pearson correlations and multivariable linear regression with HC3 robust standard errors were used. Results Knowledge was low among the 236 complete-case respondents (mean 8.13/20, SD 5.84), with 59.0% classified as having poor knowledge. Attitude was moderate (mean 33.34/50, SD 7.78), and overall practice was higher (mean 42.98/64, SD 15.83). Internal consistency was strong across domains (alpha 0.886–0.967). Attitude correlated with overall practice (r = 0.569, p < 0.001), whereas knowledge did not (r = 0.101, p = 0.120). Previous hantavirus teaching, infection prevention and control training and environmental exposure predicted higher knowledge. Attitude was the strongest independent predictor of overall practice. Conclusions Students reported comparatively frequent precautionary practice but had important disease-specific knowledge gaps, particularly regarding safe rodent-contamination cleanup, routine vaccine availability, specific antiviral treatment and person-to-person transmission. The finding that attitude, rather than knowledge, independently predicted practice suggests that scenario-based One Health education should combine factual teaching with risk appraisal, self-efficacy building, exposure-history taking and prevention counselling to strengthen future nurses’ preparedness for rodent-borne zoonotic infections. Background Hantaviruses are zoonotic viruses maintained in rodent reservoirs and are clinically important because infection, although uncommon in many settings, can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease. Human infection most often follows inhalation of aerosolised particles from infected rodent urine, droppings, saliva or nesting material, and exposure can also occur when contaminated material contacts broken skin or mucous membranes. Hantavirus ecology, epidemiology and clinical presentations vary globally, with manifestations including hantavirus pulmonary syndrome and haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome. Recent international attention to Andes virus has reinforced the need for health professionals to understand both the usual rodent-borne pathway and the rare person-to-person exception. In May 2026, WHO and CDC reported a multi-country Andes-virus-associated hantavirus cluster linked to cruise ship travel. Andes virus is the only hantavirus currently known to spread from person to person, usually through close contact with a symptomatic person, whereas most hantaviruses are not commonly transmitted between people. This distinction has direct implications for exposure-history taking, risk communication, triage, isolation decisions and infection prevention education. Prevention is largely behavioural and environmental. Current guidance emphasises reducing contact between people and rodents, sealing rodent entry points, storing food securely, avoiding dry sweeping or vacuuming of rodent-contaminated materials, dampening contaminated areas before cleaning and applying appropriate infection prevention and control (IPC) measures in health-care settings. Therefore, preparedness depends not only on generic IPC knowledge but also on disease-specific understanding of rodent reservoirs, exposure routes, safe cleanup and practical risk-reduction messages. Undergraduate nursing students are a relevant population for hantavirus preparedness. During community health learning, home visits, environmental health activities, primary care experiences and clinical placements, students may encounter patients with non-specific febrile illness, respiratory symptoms, household rodent contamination or environmental exposure histories. They therefore need sufficient understanding to ask about rodent exposure, recognise when escalation is needed, provide safe prevention advice and practise IPC under supervision. These competencies require a One Health approach rather than a purely biomedical or classroom-based IPC approach, because safe nursing responses to hantavirus depend on recognising connections between human symptoms, rodent reservoirs, household and environmental contamination, cleaning practices and community risk communication. Embedding One Health in nursing curricula can help students translate reservoir ecology and environmental exposure information into exposure-history taking, patient education, escalation decisions and IPC behaviours at the point of care. Knowledge-attitude-practice surveys can identify modifiable gaps in factual knowledge, risk perception and self-reported behaviours, although results should be interpreted with awareness of contextual and social desirability limitations. Evidence from nursing education research suggests that knowledge and attitudes can shape students’ infectious disease management behaviours. However, disease-specific evidence on nursing students’ hantavirus preparedness remains limited. This gap matters because general IPC instruction may not correct practical misconceptions about safe rodent-contamination cleanup, vaccine availability, specific antiviral treatment or overgeneralised assumptions about person-to-person transmission. The aim of this study was to examine undergraduate nursing students’ knowledge, attitudes/risk perception, clinical practices and preventive practices toward hantavirus and to identify associated factors. The objectives were to describe students’ sociodemographic and exposure characteristics; estimate levels of knowledge, attitude and practice; assess internal consistency of questionnaire domains; examine correlations among knowledge, attitude and practice; and identify independent predictors of knowledge, attitude and overall practice. Methods Study design and reporting This was a descriptive analytical cross-sectional study. The manuscript was prepared in accordance with the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) recommendations for cross-sectional studies. Setting, participants and recruitment The study was conducted among undergraduate nursing students enrolled in a nursing program at RAK Medical and Health Sciences University, Ras Al Khaimah, United Arab Emirates during the 2025–2026 academic year after ethical approval had been obtained. Eligible participants were undergraduate nursing students who provided informed consent and completed the structured questionnaire. Students who declined participation or submitted questionnaires with no valid domain responses were excluded from the study. A non-probability convenience sampling strategy was used. All eligible undergraduate nursing students enrolled in the program were approached through program-level recruitment procedures and invited to participate voluntarily. Recruitment was conducted across all years of study to maximize coverage of the student cohort. A total of 500 eligible students were invited, and 236 submitted questionnaires with valid demographic, exposure and domain-score data and were included in the complete-case analyses. analysis.background: Hantavirus infection is uncommon but can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease, making preparedness among nursing students important.
analysis.methods: A descriptive analytical cross‑sectional survey of 236 undergraduate nursing students in one program in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE during the 2025‑2026 academic year used the researcher‑developed HKAPP‑NS questionnaire; descriptive statistics, Cronbach's alpha, Pearson correlations and multivariable linear regression with HC3 robust standard errors were applied.
analysis.results: Mean knowledge was low (8.13/20 ± 5.84) with 59% classified as poor; attitude was moderate (33.34/50 ± 7.78) and practice higher (42.98/64 ± 15.83); internal consistency α 0.886‑0.967; attitude correlated with practice (r = 0.569, p < 0.001) while knowledge did not (r = 0.101, p = 0.120); prior teaching, IPC training and environmental exposure predicted higher knowledge, and attitude was the strongest independent predictor of overall practice.
analysis.contribution: The study identifies specific hantavirus knowledge gaps and shows that attitude, rather than knowledge, predicts preventive practice, informing One Health‑based curriculum enhancements for future nurses.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus infection is uncommon but can cause severe pulmonary or renal disease, requiring nursing preparedness. Methods: A descriptive analytical cross‑sectional survey of 236 undergraduate nursing students in one UAE program (2025‑2026) used a researcher‑developed HKAPP‑NS questionnaire; analysis included descriptive statistics, Cronbach’s alpha, Pearson correlations and multivariable linear regression with HC3 robust SE. Results: Mean knowledge was low (8.13/20 ± 5.84; 59% poor), attitude moderate (33.34/50 ± 7.78) and practice higher (42.98/64 ± 15.83); internal consistency α 0.886‑0.967; attitude correlated with practice (r = 0.569, p < 0.001) while knowledge did not (r = 0.101, p = 0.120); prior teaching, IPC training and exposure predicted knowledge, and attitude was the strongest independent predictor of practice. Contribution: Highlights disease‑specific knowledge gaps and that attitude drives preventive practice, guiding One‑Health curriculum enhancements for future nurses. Limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒 阿拉伯联合酋长国本科护理学生的应急准备:知识、风险感知与预防实践的横断面研究。

analysis_zh.background: 汉坦病毒 感染虽不常见,但可导致严重的肺部或肾脏疾病,因而提升护理学生的防备意识十分重要。
analysis_zh.methods: 在2025‐2026学年,对阿联酋拉斯海马的一个护理本科项目的236名本科护理学生进行描述性分析性横断面调查,使用研究者自行开发的HKAPP‐NS问卷;采用描述性统计、Cronbach α、Pearson相关以及带HC3稳健标准误的多变量线性回归进行分析。
analysis_zh.results: 平均知识水平偏低(8.13/20 ± 5.84),其中59%被归类为差;态度居中(33.34/50 ± 7.78),实践水平较高(42.98/64 ± 15.83);内部一致性α为0.886‐0.967;态度与实践呈正相关(r = 0.569, p < 0.001),而知识与实践无显著相关(r = 0.101, p = 0.120);此前的教学、IPC培训和环境暴露可预测更高的知识水平,且态度是整体实践的最强独立预测因素。
analysis_zh.contribution: 该研究识别出具体的 汉坦病毒 知识缺口,并表明态度而非知识能够预测预防性实践,为面向未来护士的One Health‐基课程改进提供依据。
analysis_zh.limitations: 在提供的证据中未报告。

papers.title: Hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome in children: a systematic scoping review of presentation, critical care management, and outcomes.
papers.abstract: Background Pediatric hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome/hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HCPS/HPS) is rare but can progress rapidly from a nonspecific febrile prodrome to respiratory failure, shock, and multiorgan dysfunction. Pediatric-specific evidence is limited, and a comprehensive map of the clinical literature is lacking. Methods We conducted a systematic scoping review per Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) guidance (protocol registered at DOI:10.17605/OSF.IO/MZDN4). PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase, Scopus, Web of Science, and Cochrane CENTRAL were searched from inception to May 2026. Eligible reports described children or adolescents (age ≤18 years) with HCPS/HPS and extractable pediatric clinical data. Two reviewers independently screened records, assessed overlap, extracted data, and appraised reporting quality using Joanna Briggs Institute tools. Results Of 2208 database records, 20 reports were included in the core pediatric synthesis after removal of 976 duplicates and screening. Reports were concentrated in the Americas. Recurrent features included fever, gastrointestinal symptoms, myalgia, thrombocytopenia, hemoconcentration, pulmonary edema, respiratory failure, and shock. Severe cases required mechanical ventilation, vasoactive support, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation, and in some cases renal replacement therapy. Larger pediatric case series and surveillance-level reports reported mortality of approximately 33% to 37%. Evidence was predominantly case-based with variable reporting completeness. Conclusions Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but potentially rapidly fatal. Early suspicion in endemic regions or after rodent exposure, with prompt supportive critical care and timely consideration of advanced cardiopulmonary support, is central to management.

Pediatric-specific evidence is limited, and a comprehensive map of the clinical literature is lacking. Methods We conducted a systematic scoping review per Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) guidance (protocol registered at DOI:10.17605/OSF.IO/MZDN4). PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase, Scopus, Web of Science, and Cochrane CENTRAL were searched from inception to May 2026. Eligible reports described children or adolescents (age ≤18 years) with HCPS/HPS and extractable pediatric clinical data. Two reviewers independently screened records, assessed overlap, extracted data, and appraised reporting quality using Joanna Briggs Institute tools. Results Of 2208 database records, 20 reports were included in the core pediatric synthesis after removal of 976 duplicates and screening. Reports were concentrated in the Americas. Recurrent features included fever, gastrointestinal symptoms, myalgia, thrombocytopenia, hemoconcentration, pulmonary edema, respiratory failure, and shock. Severe cases required mechanical ventilation, vasoactive support, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation, and in some cases renal replacement therapy. Larger pediatric case series and surveillance-level reports reported mortality of approximately 33% to 37%. Evidence was predominantly case-based with variable reporting completeness. Conclusions Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but potentially rapidly fatal. Early suspicion in endemic regions or after rodent exposure, with prompt supportive critical care and timely consideration of advanced cardiopulmonary support, is central to management.

analysis.background: Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but can progress rapidly from nonspecific fever to respiratory failure, shock, and multiorgan dysfunction, creating a need to collate scarce data (A1, A2).
analysis.main_directions: The review outlines common clinical features (fever, GI symptoms, thrombocytopenia, pulmonary edema), critical‑care interventions (mechanical ventilation, vasoactive support, ECMO, renal replacement), and stresses early suspicion with prompt supportive care (A9, A10, A14).
analysis.current_state: Across 20 reports, the described presentation and need for advanced organ support are consistent, mortality is reported at ~33‑37%, and there is agreement that rapid intensive‑care management is central (A9, A10, A11, A13).
analysis.gaps: Evidence is largely case‑based with variable reporting completeness and few pediatric‑specific studies, limiting definitive conclusions (A12, A2).
analysis.future_research: Prospective, multicenter pediatric cohorts with standardized reporting and enhanced surveillance in endemic areas are needed to clarify risk factors, optimal supportive strategies, and outcomes (A7, A12).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but can progress rapidly to fatal respiratory and circulatory failure, prompting a need to synthesize limited data (A1, A2). Main directions: The review maps clinical presentation (fever, GI symptoms, thrombocytopenia, pulmonary edema), critical‑care interventions (ventilation, vasoactive drugs, ECMO, renal replacement), and emphasizes early suspicion and supportive care (A9, A10, A14). Current state: Across 20 reports, common signs and severe organ support needs are documented, with mortality around one‑third, and consensus that prompt intensive care is essential (A9, A10, A11, A13). Gaps: Evidence is predominantly case‑based with variable completeness and few pediatric studies, limiting robust conclusions (A12, A2). Future research: Larger, prospective, standardized pediatric cohorts and enhanced surveillance in endemic regions are required to define risk factors, optimal supportive strategies, and outcomes (A7, A12).

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒心肺综合征 在儿童中的研究:关于表现、危重症管理与结局的系统性范围综述

analysis_zh.background: 儿童汉坦病毒心肺综合征(HCPS)/肺综合征(HPS)罕见,但可从非特异性发热迅速进展至呼吸衰竭、休克和多器官功能障碍,因而需要汇总稀缺数据 (A1, A2)。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 本综述概述了常见临床特征(发热、胃肠道症状、血小板减少、肺水肿)、重症监护干预措施(机械通气、血管活性药物支持、体外膜氧合(ECMO)、肾脏替代治疗),并强调需早期怀疑并及时提供支持治疗 (A9, A10, A14)。
analysis_zh.current_state: 在 20 份报告中,所描述的表现和对高级器官支持的需求是一致的,据报告死亡率约为 33‐37%,并且一致认为快速重症监护管理是核心(A9、A10、A11、A13)。
analysis_zh.gaps: 证据主要基于病例,报告完整性差异大,且儿童特异性研究稀少,限制了明确结论的得出 (A12, A2)。
analysis_zh.future_research: 需要前瞻性、多中心儿童队列研究,采用标准化报告并在流行地区加强监测,以阐明风险因素、最佳支持策略及结局 (A7, A12)。

papers.summary_zh: 背景:儿童汉坦病毒心肺综合征(HCPS)/肺综合征(HPS)罕见,但可从非特异性发热迅速进展至呼吸衰... 主要方向:本综述概述了常见临床特征(发热、胃肠道症状、血小板减少、肺水肿)、重症监护干预措施(机械通气、... 研究现状:在 20 份报告中,所描述的表现和对高级器官支持的需求是一致的,据报告死亡率约为 33‐37%... 不足:证据主要基于病例,报告完整性差异大,且儿童特异性研究稀少,限制了明确结论的得出 (A12, A... 后续研究:需要前瞻性、多中心儿童队列研究,采用标准化报告并在流行地区加强监测,以阐明风险因素、最佳支持策...

papers.title: Ribavirin post-exposure prophylaxis for Andes virus exposure: a viewpoint.
papers.abstract: Andes virus (ANDV) is unique among hantaviruses because person-to-person transmission is possible. This raises questions on the relevance of post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP), particularly following high-risk household, healthcare-associated, or laboratory exposures, considering its high case fatality rate. Ribavirin has demonstrated antiviral activity against hantaviruses in vitro and in animal models, although clinical evidence supporting its use for ANDV remains extremely limited. This viewpoint summarises the currently available evidence regarding ribavirin as PEP after potential ANDV exposure. We discuss the knowledge gaps that may limit the applicability of ribavirin PEP, to make informed decisions on the use of ribavirin in the setting of PEP. Potential dosing strategies are visualised by modelling and simulation, and potential dose and duration are discussed. Although the biological rationale for ribavirin PEP appears compelling, absence of controlled human studies and potential toxicity currently limit its role to highly selected exposure scenarios and use shortly after exposure.

analysis.background: Andes virus uniquely transmits between humans and has a high case fatality, prompting interest in post‑exposure prophylaxis.
analysis.main_directions: The viewpoint reviews ribavirin’s antiviral rationale, potential dosing regimens derived from modelling, and the limited clinical data.
analysis.current_state: Ribavirin shows in‑vitro and animal efficacy against hantaviruses, but human evidence for ANDV PEP is extremely scarce and toxicity is a concern.
analysis.gaps: There are no controlled human trials, optimal dose and duration are uncertain, and safety concerns limit use to selected high‑risk exposures.
analysis.future_research: Controlled clinical studies to define efficacy, safety, and dosing of ribavirin PEP, supported by pharmacokinetic modelling and systematic surveillance of ANDV exposures.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus uniquely transmits between humans and has a high case fatality, prompting interest in post‑exposure prophylaxis. Main directions: the viewpoint reviews ribavirin’s antiviral rationale, potential dosing regimens derived from modelling, and the limited clinical data. Current state: ribavirin shows in‑vitro and animal efficacy against hantaviruses, but human evidence for ANDV PEP is extremely scarce and toxicity is a concern. Gaps: there are no controlled human trials, optimal dose and duration are uncertain, and safety concerns limit use to selected high‑risk exposures. Future research: controlled clinical studies to define efficacy, safety, and dosing of ribavirin PEP, supported by pharmacokinetic modelling and systematic surveillance of ANDV exposures.

papers.title_zh: 利巴韦林暴露后预防[ PDI0008 ]: 观点

analysis_zh.background: 安第斯病毒 在人际间独特传播,且致死率高,因而引起对暴露后预防的关注。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 本文回顾利巴韦林的抗病毒原理、基于模型推导的潜在给药方案以及有限的临床数据。
analysis_zh.current_state: 利巴韦林在体外及动物模型中对 汉坦病毒 显示有效,但针对 ANDV 暴露后预防的人体证据极其稀缺,且毒性是一个关注点。
analysis_zh.gaps: 尚无对照的人体试验,最佳剂量和疗程尚不明确,安全性顾虑使其仅限于特定高危暴露人群使用。
analysis_zh.future_research: 开展对照临床研究以明确利巴韦林暴露后预防的疗效、安全性和剂量方案,并结合药代动力学模型及对 ANDV 暴露的系统监测。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：安第斯病毒 在人际间独特传播,且致死率高,因而引起对暴露后预防的关注。 主要方向：本文回顾利巴韦林的抗病毒原理、基于模型推导的潜在给药方案以及有限的临床数据。 研究现状：利巴韦林在体外及动物模型中对 汉坦病毒 显示有效,但针对 ANDV 暴露后预防的人体证据极其稀缺,且毒性是一个关注点。 不足：尚无对照的人体试验,最佳剂量和疗程尚不明确,安全性顾虑使其仅限于特定高危暴露人群使用。 后续研究：开展对照临床研究以明确利巴韦林暴露后预防的疗效、安全性和剂量方案,并结合药代动力学模型及对 ANDV 暴露的系统监测。

papers.title: t-PA/PAI-1 complex expression correlates with the severity in hantaan virus-induced hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome.
papers.abstract: OBJECTIVES: This study aimed to investigate t-PAIC concentration changes across severity levels in HFRS patients and evaluate its value in predicting disease severity, providing a potential biomarker for HFRS severity assessment and monitoring. METHODS: t-PAIC levels were measured by chemiluminescence immunoassay in 156 HFRS patients and 37 healthy controls. Routine laboratory tests assessed WBC, PLT, Cr, UA, urea, HDL, ALB, APTT, and PT. HFRS-specific antibodies were detected using the colloidal gold method. Spearman correlation analysis evaluated associations between t-PAIC and clinical indices. Ordinal logistic regression identified risk factors linked to disease severity. ROC curve analysis assessed the predictive performance of t-PAIC for HFRS severity. RESULTS: t-PAIC levels progressively increased with HFRS severity, peaking in critically ill patients. t-PAIC showed significant positive correlations with leukocyte count (r = 0.6082; p < 0.01), Cr (r = 0.3567; p < 0.01), urea (r = 0.4376; p < 0.01), UA (r = 0.3201; p < 0.01), APTT (r = 0.6182; p < 0.01), and PT (r = 0.2997; p < 0.01), and significant inverse correlations with PLT (r = -0.6177; p < 0.01), HDL (r = -0.3074; p < 0.01), and ALB (r = -0.4790; p < 0.01). ROC analysis revealed t-PAIC had good predictive value for disease severity (AUC = 0.796, 95% CI: 0.724-0.867, p < 0.001). CONCLUSIONS: t-PAIC levels closely reflect HFRS severity and associated clinical features, including renal dysfunction and coagulation abnormalities, suggesting its potential as a biomarker for monitoring disease status.
papers.journal: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology

TYPE Original Research PUBLISHED 08 July 2026 DOI 10.3389/fcimb.2026.1849586 Introduction Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS), an ancient yet recurrent infectious disease, continues to pose a significant threat to public health and has garnered increasing global attention. The earliest recorded description of this disease dates back to a Chinese medical text from 960 AD (Noh et al., 2019). In modern times, HFRS was first reported in South Korea in 1951 during the Korean War. However, the causative agent, Hantaan virus (HTNV), re- mained unidentified until 1976 and was subsequently confirmed by researchers in 1978 (Smadel, 1953; French et al., 1981). HTNV is a zoonotic, negative-sense RNA virus belonging to the family Elliovirales within the order Bunyaviricetes (Walker et al., 2019; (ICTV), 2026). The striped field mouse (Apodemus agrarius) serves as the natural reservoir host of this virus, typically exhibiting an asymptomatic infection. Humans, however, can become infected through inhalation of aerosols contaminated with excreta—such as saliva, urine, and feces—from infected rodents. Initial symptoms are often nonspecific and resemble influenza, including fever and headache. These may progress to more severe manifestations such as hypotension, thrombocytopenia, and renal impairment. In severe cases, patients may develop multiple organ dysfunction syndrome (MODS) (Avsic-Zupanc et al., 2019; Vaheri et al., 2013a; Muranyi et al., 2005). Furthermore, comorbidities affecting the renal, car- diovascular, and endocrine systems have been reported following HTNV infection (Vaheri et al., 2013b). HFRS is endemic across Europe and Asia. China bears a substantial disease burden, ac- counting for approximately 90% of global HFRS cases. Between 1950 and 2014, a total of 1,625,002 HFRS cases and 46,968 HFRS- related deaths were documented in China. In 2021 alone, 2,657 HFRS cases were reported in Shaanxi Province, coinciding with the ongoing period of the COVID-19 pandemic (Yan et al., 2007; Wei et al., 2021). With increasing insights into the pathogenesis of this disorder, capillary and small vessel endothelial cells are now recognized as the primary targets of hantaviruses, and enhanced vascular permeability represents a hallmark feature of the patho- logical alterations observed in HFRS (Noack et al., 2020). Although integrated interventions—including rodent control, environmental management, and vaccination—have substantially reduced the incidence of HFRS, the persistent or re-emergent transmission in endemic regions poses ongoing challenges for disease surveillance (Liu et al., 2019). Therefore, identifying a reliable clinical parameter to reflect the disease status of HFRS patients is of critical impor- tance, as it would also facilitate severity assessment and prognosis prediction in clinical practice. The t-PA/PAI-1 complex (t-PAIC) consists of tissue plasmin- ogen activator (tPA) and plasminogen activator inhibitor 1 (PAI-1). The former is a 68-kDa serine protease that was initially purified and characterized in human circulation and later in the uterus in 1979, while the latter is a single-chain 45-kDa glycoprotein com- posed of 379 or 381 amino acids, belonging to the serine proteinase inhibitor superfamily, and was first reported in 1986 (Suzuki et al., 2009; Yasar Yildiz et al., 2014). PAI-1 can be produced by various cell types and tissues, including vascular smooth muscle cells, adipocytes, splenocytes, and hepatocytes (Tsantes et al., 2008). Notably, both PAI-1 and tPA are secreted by endothelial cells— the primary target of HTNV infection, as previously mentioned— suggesting a potential role of t-PAIC in the pathogenesis of HFRS (Noack et al., 2020; Suzuki et al., 2009; Tsantes et al., 2008). Indeed, recent studies have demonstrated that t-PAIC serves as an impor- tant biomarker in venous thrombosis, with levels markedly elevated prior to thrombotic events. Moreover, plasma concentrations of this complex have been significantly associated with the risk of myo- cardial infarction in both men and women (Nordenhem et al., 2005). In addition, t-PAIC levels are considered an independent predictor of all-cause and cardiovascular mortality in patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (Winter et al., 2017). Moreover, fluctuations in this complex have been closely associated with postoperative bleeding following cardiac surgery; furthermore, t-PAIC has been proposed as a potential biomarker for the early diagnosis, assessment, and prognosis of sepsis-induced coagulopa- thy (Ozolina et al., 2012; Li et al., 2023). Given that HFRS patients exhibit an increased risk of coagulation disorders—evidenced by a higher incidence of disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), acute myocardial infarction (AMI), and stroke—and considering the critical roles of t-PA and PAI-1 in regulating plasmin within the fibrinolytic system (Suzuki et al., 2009; Tsantes et al., 2008; Chen et al., 2024), the association between t-PAIC levels and the severity of Hantaan virus-induced HFRS remains to be fully elucidated. Accordingly, in this study, we examined t-PAIC levels across different stages of HFRS; analyzed the associations between this complex and markers of renal function, coagulation, and inflam- mation; and evaluated the correlation between fluctuations in t- PAIC concentrations and the clinical severity of HFRS. Furthermore, the predictive value of t-PAIC for disease severity was assessed using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis. 2 Materials and methods 2.1 Study population This study is a cross-sectional, single-center research that initially recruited 212 HFRS patients who were hospitalized at the Department of Infectious Diseases of Shaanxi Provincial Hospital of Infectious Diseases (The Xi’an Eighth Hospital) from November 2023 to May 2025 as candidates, and finally 156 patients who meet the criteria described below were enrolled with a correspondence 37 healthy individuals as controls (Figure 1). Specifically, patients were recruited according to the HFRS criteria for clinical diagnosis and classification, released by the National Health Commission of China which included (China, 2008): (i) epidemiological history that travel or living in an epidemic region, or have a record of direct or indirect contacts with rodent excreta, (ii) clinical manifestations including fever, fatigue, nausea, vomiting, hemorrhage, hypotensive shock, or kidney impairment. (iii) positive serum-specific antibodies for Hantaan virus. Meanwhile, the patients were excluded based on the criteria as follows: (i) viral hepatitis or other viral infection, (ii) autoimmune diseases, (iii) hematological diseases, (iv) patients who suffered tumors and were subjected to radiotherapy and Zhao et al. 10.3389/fcimb.2026.1849586 Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology frontiersin.org 02 chemotherapy. The clinical information and laboratory testing data were collected and analyzed anonymously, and informed consent was provided by the participants. All procedures were performed as the ethical standards outlined in the 1964 Helsinki Declaration and its subsequent amendments or comparable ethical standards and were approved by the Institutional Review Board of Shaanxi Provincial Hospital of Infectious Disease. 2.2 Definition The severity of HFRS patients was categorized into four subgroups based on the clinical manifestations and disease pro- gression: mild cases (n=39) were characterized by renal injury without significant hypotension or oliguria; moderate cases (n=42) presented with hypotension, skin and mucous membrane hemorrhages, bulbar conjunctival congestion, and acute kidney injury (AKI) accompanied by oliguria; severe cases (n=38) exhibited marked uremia, persistent hypotension, skin and mucous mem- brane bleeding, bulbar conjunctival edema, peritoneal or pleural effusion, and AKI defined by a daily urine output of 50–500 mL for up to 5 days, or a reduction in urine output to less than 100 mL per day for no more than 2 days; critical cases (n=37) demonstrated one or more life-threatening complications, including refractory shock lasting at least 2 days, severe AKI with a daily urine output of 50– 500 mL for up to 5 days or less than 100 mL per day for up to 2 days, severe secondary infection or heart failure, visceral hemorrhage, or pulmonary and cerebral edema. In addition, the clinical course was also divided into the acute phase—which encompasses the febrile, hypotensive, and oliguric stages—and the convalescent phase— which includes the diuretic and convalescent stages—based on the criteria outlined above by the National Health Commission of China (China, 2008). 2.3 Examination of routine laboratory parameters Peripheral blood was collected from the venous circulation of both patients and healthy individuals, followed by centrifugation at 537 relative centrifugal force (g) (TD4A, Changsha Yingtai Instrument, China) for 10 minutes at 4 °C. Ten laboratory param- eters were measured using automated analyzers as per standard protocols (Mindray SAL9000, Mindray BC6900, Mindray ExC810; Mindray Medical, China), including white blood cell (WBC) count, platelet (PLT) count, activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT), prothrombin time (PT), fibrinogen, creatinine (Cr), urea, uric acid (UA), high-density lipoprotein (HDL), and albumin (ALB). 2.4 Examination of HTNV antibody The colloidal gold method was employed to detect antibody titers specific for HTNV according to the manufacturer’s instruc- tions (Bosheng Biotechnology, China). First, 2 μL of EDTA-K2 anticoagulated plasma was added to 100 μL of sample dilution solution; subsequently, 70 μL of the mixture was transferred onto the test device and incubated for 20 min as per the prescribed protocol. Results were interpreted within 15 min. The assay kits exhibited a sensitivity of 96.71% and a specificity of 98.72%. 2.5 Examination of t-PA/PAI-1 complex Venous blood samples were collected from both HFRS patients and healthy individuals and transferred into sodium citrate anticoag- ulant tubes, followed by a 10-minute incubation at 37 °C. Subsequently, plasma was separated by centrifugation at 1209 relative centrifugal force (g) (TD4A, Changsha Yingtai Instrument, China) for 10 minutes. FIGURE 1 Flowchart of the study. t-PAIC, t-PA/PAI-1 complex; HFRS, hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome; ROC, receiver operating characteristics curve; WBC, white blood cell; PLT, Platelets; Cr, Creatinine; UA, Uric acid; APTT, Activated partial thromboplastin time; PT, Prothrombin time; HDL, High- density lipoprotein; ALB, Albumin. Zhao et al. 10.3389/fcimb.2026.1849586 Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology frontiersin.org 03 The concentrations of the t-PA/PAI-1 complex were determined using the chemiluminescence immunoassay (CLIA) method on an auto- mated analyzer according to the manufacturer’s instructions (Wondfo I2900, Wondfo Bio, China). In brief, 10 μL of plasma and 50 μL of magnetic bead working solution were added to the reaction vessel and incubated for 5 minutes at 37 °C, then washed. Next, 50 μL of alkaline phosphatase (ALP)-labeled working solution was added and incubated at 37 °C for another 5 minutes. Luminescence signals emitted from the double-antibody sandwich complex—comprising magnetic beads coated with antibody, the t-PA/PAI-1 complex, and ALP-labeled antibody—were subsequently measured at a wavelength of 470 nm. 2.6 Statistical analysis Figures were generated using Origin 2021 software (OriginLab Corporation, USA), while tables were prepared using Excel 2021 (Microsoft Corporation, USA). Statistical analyses were performed with GraphPad Prism 8.0 software (GraphPad Software, LLC, USA). Statistics values are presented as medians with interquartile ranges (IQR) and were analyzed using the Kruskal-Wallis H test, as appropriate. Spearman’s correlation analysis was employed to evaluate the association between the t-PA/PAI-1 complex and multiple conventional laboratory parameters. The risk factors for disease severity were assessed through ordinal logistic regression analysis, and the predictive performance of the t-PAIC for the severity of HFRS was evaluated using receiver operating character- istic (ROC) curve analysis, with results reported as the area under the ROC curve (AUC), the combined models were constructed using logistic regression-derived probabilities. A P value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. 3 Results 3.1 General information and characteristics of HFRS patients Although 212 laboratory-confirmed HFRS patients were initially considered eligible for this study, individuals with concomitant viral hepatitis or other viral infections, autoimmune disorders, hematolog- ical diseases, tumors, or those who had undergone radiotherapy or chemotherapy were excluded based on the enrollment criteria. As a result, a total of 156 HFRS patients were enrolled and classified according to the guidelines issued by the National Health Commission of China. The patient distribution comprised 39 mild cases, 42 moderate cases, 38 severe cases, and 37 critical cases. As shown in Table 1, male patients predominated across all disease severity groups, and hospitalization duration progressively increased with the worsening of clinical condition. Furthermore, a consistent upward trend was observed in several conventional laboratory param- eters, including WBC, Cr, urea, uric acid UA, and APTT. In contrast, indices such as PLT, HDL, and ALB exhibited a marked decline that correlated with disease progression. Notably, significantly reduced Fib levels were observed only in critical-type patients, with no significant reduction detected in mild, moderate, or severe cases. 3.2 Association between t-PAIC fluctuation and the severity of HFRS The concentrations of t-PAIC were measured across different subgroups to explore the association between t-PAIC level changes and the progression of HFRS. As illustrated in Figure 2, a significant TABLE 1 Baseline demographics and clinical characteristics of patients with hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome. Between 1950 and 2014, a total of 1,625,002 HFRS cases and 46,968 HFRS- related deaths were documented in China. In 2021 alone, 2,657 HFRS cases were reported in Shaanxi Province, coinciding with the ongoing period of the COVID-19 pandemic (Yan et al., 2007; Wei et al., 2021). With increasing insights into the pathogenesis of this disorder, capillary and small vessel endothelial cells are now recognized as the primary targets of hantaviruses, and enhanced vascular permeability represents a hallmark feature of the patho- logical alterations observed in HFRS (Noack et al., 2020). Although integrated interventions—including rodent control, environmental management, and vaccination—have substantially reduced the incidence of HFRS, the persistent or re-emergent transmission in endemic regions poses ongoing challenges for disease surveillance (Liu et al., 2019). Therefore, identifying a reliable clinical parameter to reflect the disease status of HFRS patients is of critical impor- tance, as it would also facilitate severity assessment and prognosis prediction in clinical practice. The t-PA/PAI-1 complex (t-PAIC) consists of tissue plasmin- ogen activator (tPA) and plasminogen activator inhibitor 1 (PAI-1). The former is a 68-kDa serine protease that was initially purified and characterized in human circulation and later in the uterus in 1979, while the latter is a single-chain 45-kDa glycoprotein com- posed of 379 or 381 amino acids, belonging to the serine proteinase inhibitor superfamily, and was first reported in 1986 (Suzuki et al., 2009; Yasar Yildiz et al., 2014). PAI-1 can be produced by various cell types and tissues, including vascular smooth muscle cells, adipocytes, splenocytes, and hepatocytes (Tsantes et al., 2008). Notably, both PAI-1 and tPA are secreted by endothelial cells— the primary target of HTNV infection, as previously mentioned— suggesting a potential role of t-PAIC in the pathogenesis of HFRS (Noack et al., 2020; Suzuki et al., 2009; Tsantes et al., 2008). Indeed, recent studies have demonstrated that t-PAIC serves as an impor- tant biomarker in venous thrombosis, with levels markedly elevated prior to thrombotic events. Moreover, plasma concentrations of this complex have been significantly associated with the risk of myo- cardial infarction in both men and women (Nordenhem et al., 2005). In addition, t-PAIC levels are considered an independent predictor of all-cause and cardiovascular mortality in patie…

analysis.background: The study investigates how t-PAIC concentrations vary with hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) severity and evaluates its potential as a predictive biomarker.
analysis.methods: 156 HFRS patients and 37 healthy controls were enrolled; t-PAIC was measured by chemiluminescence immunoassay, routine labs (WBC, PLT, Cr, UA, urea, HDL, ALB, APTT, PT) and HFRS antibodies were assessed; Spearman correlation, ordinal logistic regression, and ROC analysis were performed.
analysis.results: t-PAIC levels rose progressively with HFRS severity, peaking in critically ill patients; significant positive correlations were found with leukocyte count (r=0.6082), Cr (r=0.3567), urea (r=0.4376), UA (r=0.3201), APTT (r=0.6182), PT (r=0.2997); inverse correlations with PLT (r=−0.6177), HDL (r=−0.3074), ALB (r=−0.4790); ROC AUC for severity prediction was 0.796 (95% CI 0.724–0.867).
analysis.contribution: t-PAIC closely reflects HFRS severity and associated renal dysfunction and coagulation abnormalities, indicating its potential as a biomarker for disease monitoring.
analysis.limitations: not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The study investigates how t-PAIC concentrations vary with hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) severity and evaluates its potential as a predictive biomarker. Methods: 156 HFRS patients and 37 healthy controls were enrolled; t-PAIC was measured by chemiluminescence immunoassay, routine labs and HFRS antibodies were assessed; Spearman correlation, ordinal logistic regression, and ROC analysis were performed. Results: t-PAIC levels rose progressively with HFRS severity, peaking in critically ill patients; significant positive correlations were found with leukocyte count, Cr, urea, UA, APTT, PT; inverse correlations with PLT, HDL, ALB; ROC AUC for severity prediction was 0.796 (95% CI 0.724–0.867). Contribution: t-PAIC closely reflects HFRS severity and associated renal dysfunction and coagulation abnormalities, indicating its potential as a biomarker for disease monitoring. Limitations: not reported in the supplied evidence.

papers.title_zh: t-PA/PAI-1 复合物表达与 汉滩病毒 诱导的 肾综合征出血热 严重程度相关。

analysis_zh.background: 本研究调查 t-PAIC 浓度随 肾综合征出血热(HFRS)严重程度的变化,并评估其作为预测性生物标志物的潜力。
analysis_zh.methods: 纳入 156 例 HFRS 患者和 37 例健康对照;采用化学发光免疫测定法测定 t-PAIC,评估常规实验室指标(WBC、PLT、Cr、UA、urea、HDL、ALB、APTT、PT)及 HFRS 抗体;进行 Spearman 相关、序数逻辑回归和 ROC 分析。
analysis_zh.results: t-PAIC 水平随 HFRS 严重程度逐步升高,在危重患者中达到峰值;与白细胞计数 (r=0.6082)、Cr (r=0.3567)、尿素 (r=0.4376)、UA (r=0.3201)、APTT (r=0.6182)、PT (r=0.2997) 呈显著正相关;与 PLT (r=−0.6177)、HDL (r=−0.3074)、ALB (r=−0.4790) 呈负相关;用于严重程度预测的 ROC AUC 为 0.796(95% CI 0.724–0.867)。
analysis_zh.contribution: t-PAIC 能够准确反映 HFRS 的严重程度及相关的肾功能损害和凝血异常,提示其在疾病监测中的生物标志物潜力。
analysis_zh.limitations: 未在所提供证据中报告。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：本研究调查 t-PAIC 浓度随 肾综合征出血热(HFRS)严重程度的变化,并评估其作为预测性生物标志物的潜力。 方法：纳入 156 例 HFRS 患者和 37 例健康对照;采用化学发光免疫测定法测定 t-PAIC,评估常规实验室指标(WBC、PLT、Cr、UA… 结果：t-PAIC 水平随 HFRS 严重程度逐步升高,在危重患者中达到峰值;与白细胞计数 (r=0.6082)、Cr (r=0.3567)、尿素… 贡献：t-PAIC 能够准确反映 HFRS 的严重程度及相关的肾功能损害和凝血异常,提示其在疾病监测中的生物标志物潜力。 局限：未在所提供证据中报告。
